pointing his finger towards the male’s face and aggressively tossing the basket where citizens place their personal items (General Police Order 2.01.02 and Manual of Rules 2.02, 2.03 and

Charge & Discipline Decision:
Specifications: Failed to correctly complete and submit an OH-1 crash report in a timely manner
(×8) (General Police Order 8.1.02 and Manual of Rules 9.08 and 9.12) – Observed a Motor
Vehicle Accident and delayed in correctly completing and submitting an OH-1 crash report,
causing it to be delivered to the Record File Section late (General Police Order 8.1.02 and
Manual of Rules 9.08 and 9.12) – Failed to properly categorize Wearable Camera System (WCS)
videos in nine incidents (General Police Order 4.06.04) – Sick leave abuse in the First Quarter
of 2023 (General Police Order 1.3.06).

Disposition: Found “Guilty” of eleven Group I violations. Considering the officer’s active
discipline history (none), the officer received a five-day suspension. This corrective action is
within the Group I violation range of the discipline matrix when aggravating factors (multiple
violations arising from a single event or incident may escalate the disciplinary action to the
maximum group range, delay in reporting further victimizes citizens) outweigh mitigating factors
(no active discipline, accepted responsibility). In addition, the officer received a Non-
disciplinary Letter of Reinstruction placing him on Step-1 of the Sick Leave Abuse Control
Program until September 6, 2024.

Rationale: The officer pled “No Contest” and accepted responsibility for failing to complete
nine OH-1 Crash Reports in a timely manner and correctly categorizing them in Evidence.com.
In addition, the officer exhibited a pattern of sick time abuse, to wit: during the First Quarter of
2023, he used 63 hours of undocumented sick leave with 32 hours taken on weekends and the
other 31 hours attached to those weekend hours. Additionally, during the two-month look-back
period, he used another 24 hours of undocumented sick leave, of which 16 hours were on
weekends.
Charges: Sick Leave Abuse,WCS Violation,Failure to Report/Notify,Lack of Service

Specifications: Conducted Ohio Law Enforcement Gateway (OHLEG) inquiries transgressing
policy as follows: a). On or about May 30, 2016, while off-duty, the officer conducted inquiries
on himself and personal family members, b). On or about August 31, 2016, while on duty, the
officer conducted inquiries on himself, c). On or about October 30, 2016, while on duty, the
officer conducted inquiries on himself (General Police Order 1.3.02 and OHLEG Security
Protocols) – Did diminish the esteem of the Division of Police when, after meeting a female
citizen on a radio assignment, did contact a female and ask her on a date, which led to a sexual
relationship as recent as 2021 (Manual of Rules 5.01) – Contacted female citizens after radio
assignments and went on dates with them (General Police Order 1.07.06).
Disposition: Found “Guilty” of three Group III violations. The officer was terminated from
employment with the City of Cleveland, Department of Public Safety, Division of Police. This
discipline is within the First Group III violation range of the discipline matrix when aggravating
factors (multiple violations arising from a single event or incident may escalate the disciplinary
action to the maximum group range) outweigh mitigating factors (no active discipline, accepted
responsibility).
Rationale: The officer exhibited a complete disregard for ethical behavior and admitted to
contacting multiple women that were met during radio assignments along with other occasions
on-duty and used their personal information obtained as a police officer to contact them and ask
them on dates. This conduct significantly compromises the reputation of the Division.

Specification: Failed to properly identify the correct suspect when issuing a warrant, causing a
mistakenly identified citizen to be held in jail for thirty-six hours. The officer was provided the
correct identifying suspect information via the Cuyahoga County Metropolitan Housing
Authority Police Department incident report, and did not take the extra step to obtain and verify
additional suspect information with the victim, in person, prior to issuing a warrant for the
mistakenly identified suspect. This resulted in the court case being dismissed by the City of
Cleveland Prosecutor’s Office. This conduct constitutes the crime of Dereliction of Duty in the
State of Ohio under ORC 2921.44(B), a misdemeanor of the 2nd degree (Manual of Rules 2.01,
4.03 and 4.17) – Failed to ensure the prosecutor was aware of all the elements present for the
crime of Domestic Violence causing that crime to be omitted from the Misdemeanor Review
Form (Manual of Rules 4.03) – The officer hand wrote “Domestic Violence” on a Misdemeanor
Review Form without consulting the Reviewing Prosecutor, causing an arrest warrant to be
improperly served against a suspect which resulted in the court cases being dismissed by the City
of Cleveland Prosecutor’s Office. In addition, this caused inability for the officer to work as a
Cleveland Division of Police representative to the City Prosecutor’s Office and tarnished the
reputation of Cleveland Division of Police members with the County Public Defender Office
(Manual of Rules 4.17) – Caused the Cleveland Division of Police to be the focus of negative
media attention stemming from the officer’s failure to properly investigate two Domestic
Violence cases (Manual of Rules 5.01)

Disposition: Found “Guilty” of two Group II Violations and two Group III violations. On May
25, 2023, the officer was terminated from employment with the City of Cleveland, Department
of Public Safety, Division of Police. This corrective action is within the First Group III violation
range of the discipline matrix when aggravating factors (multiple violations arising from a single
event or incident may escalate the disciplinary action to the maximum group range) outweigh
mitigating factors (no active discipline).

Rationale: The officer failed to properly identify the correct suspect when issuing a warrant
which caused a mistakenly identified citizen to be held in jail for thirty-six hours; when the

Domestic Violence causing that crime to be omitted from the Misdemeanor Review form; and
when as a result of the officers actions, the Cleveland Division of Police became the focus of
negative media attention.

Specification: Receive two Uniform Traffic Tickets in the City of Cleveland, for violating CCO
433.02A Operation in Willful or Wanton Disregard of Safety and CCO 435.07A Driving Under
Suspension or Revocation, subsequently found guilty of the amended charge of 435.01 License
Required to Operate (Manual of Rules 2.01) – Operated a Cleveland Division of Police patrol car
for eighteen tours of duty while having a suspended Driver License (Manual of Rules 2.01) –
Failed to follow the guidelines of behavior when off-duty and in plain clothes while being
challenged by an on-duty police officer taking a police action (General Police Order 3.4.23) –
Did have unauthorized ammunition in his service weapon while on-duty (General Police Order
1.3.11) – Was untruthful in his Garrity interview, that a vehicle’s title was given when he
received a bike. While initially on scene, he contradicted that statement multiple times, all
recorded on a Cleveland Police Wearable Camera System (Manual of Rules 3.12) – Was
untruthful when he made statements about his interaction with an on-duty officer, referring to
statements that the on-duty officer never made (Manual of Rules 3.12) – Failed to maintain a
valid Driver License and did not notify a supervisor of the change to his Driver License status
(General Police Order 1.3.05) – Was charged in Parma Municipal Court for ORC 2921.13(A)(5)
Falsification (M1) and ORC 4505.03 Certificate of Title (M4). Subsequently, he pled guilty to,
and was found guilty of an amended charge of Parma Codified Ordinance 606.14 (M2)
Obstructing Official Business (Manual of Rules 2.01) – Was charged in Parma Municipal Court,
for ORC 2921.13 (A)(5) Falsification (M1). Subsequently he pled guilty to, and was found guilty
of an amended charge of Parma Codified Ordinance 606.14 (M2) Obstructing Official Business
(Manual of Rules 2.01) – Used a Cleveland, Ohio address for all Cleveland Division of Police
records, knowing that he did not reside at this address and failed to notify his superior officer in
writing of the change to his correct home address (Manual of Rules 2.12) – Knowingly made
false statements on Bureau of Motor Vehicle forms by using the Cleveland Division of Police
Fifth District Headquarters address as his residential address. He was criminally charged by the
Bureau of Motor Vehicles for ORC 2913.31 Forgery and ORC 2913.42 Tampering with
Records, and subjected to adjudication in the Cuyahoga County Court of Common Pleas.
Furthermore, he went to the same Bureau of Motor Vehicles and registered two other vehicles in
the same manner. –– Found Guilty on two counts of Tampering with Records (F3) and two
counts of Forgery (F5). As a result of his criminal convictions, the esteem of the Division has
been reasonably diminished in the eyes of the public (Manual of Rules 5.01).


Disposition: Found “Guilty” of one Group I violation, two Group II violations and ten Group III
violations. On August 31, 2023, the officer was terminated from employment with the City of
Cleveland, Department of Public Safety, Division of Police. This corrective action is within the
First Group III violation range of the discipline matrix when aggravating factors (multiple
violations arising from a single event or incident may escalate the disciplinary action to the
maximum group range, active discipline) outweigh mitigating factors (none).
Rationale: The officer was untruthful during his Garrity interview and when making a statement
to a Cleveland Police Sergeant. In addition, he violated numerous State criminal codes, City of
Cleveland criminal codes and City of Parma criminal codes, as well as various General Police
Orders and Manual of Rules of the City of Cleveland, Division of Police. The officer was found
guilty in courts of law of several criminal violations, including four felonies and imprisoned.
Charges: Untruthfulness,Conduct Unbecoming / Diminished Esteem,Arrest / Criminal Charge

Charge & Discipline Decision:
Specifications: While off-duty, in the Fourth District Lobby, engaged in conduct that would
constitute the crime of Assault (Cleveland Codified Ordinance 621.03 and Manual of Rules
2.01) – While off-duty, in the Fourth District Lobby, initiated a verbal altercation that turned into
a physical altercation with a citizen who was at the District to file a crime report (Manual of
Rules 5.01) – While off-duty, in the Fourth District Lobby, engaged in a face to face verbal
altercation where she made comments such as “I don’t have to reason with you,” “you can have a
seat or leave,” and “I do not have to do anything for you” (General Police Order 2.01.02) –
While off-duty, in the Fourth District Lobby, slapped a cell phone out of a citizen’s hand while
the citizen was recording their interaction (General Police Order 4.08.05).

Disposition: Found “Guilty” of two Group I violations and two Group III violations.
Considering the member’s active discipline history [5/28/2021: Arbitration Award 2/15/2022:
fourteen-day suspension (5/28/2021: Terminated)], the member was terminated from
employment with the City of Cleveland, Department of Public Safety, Division of Police
effective Friday, January 12, 2024. This corrective action is within the Group III violation range
of the discipline matrix when aggravating factors (multiple violations arising from a single event
or incident may escalate the disciplinary action to the maximum group range, active discipline)
outweigh mitigating factors (accepted partial responsibility).

Rationale: The officer responded to the Fourth District lobby in civilian clothing and failed to
identify herself as a police officer when she proceeded to engage in a close quarters, face to face,
verbal altercation with a citizen. At times, with another on-duty officer standing in between the
officer and the citizen, disregarding the importance and safety of maintaining physical distance,
she continued her display of inappropriate behavior when she made comments such as “I don’t
have to reason with you,” “you can have a seat or leave,” and “I do not have to do anything for
you.” The officer disregarded de-escalation training and tactics when she continued to aggravate
the circumstances, escalated the incident and became the aggressor. The officer slapped a cell
phone out of a citizen’s hand and struck the citizen in the face with an open hand (with another
on-duty officer in between the officer and the citizen). This conduct would constitute the crime
of “Assault” and unbecoming of a member of the Department of Public Safety. The officer
ignored efforts by other officers working to de-escalate the situation and separate her from the
citizen. As officers were physically trying to restrain and separate the officer from the citizen,
she continued her attempts to reach the citizen, jeopardizing the safety of everyone who was
involved to include the citizen and other officers. Multiple violations arising from a single event
or incident may escalate the disciplinary action to the maximum group range.
Charges: Conduct Unbecoming / Diminished Esteem,Violence in the Workplace

Charge & Discipline Decision:
Specifications: Improperly accessed another officer’s information via the OHLEG law
enforcement database without a legitimate law enforcement purpose. (General Police Orders
1.1.39 and 7.03.02 and Manual of Rules 2.01, 9.14 and 9.15) – Knowingly accessed a
coworker’s LERMS report information without a law enforcement purpose (General Police
Order 7.03.02 and Manual of Rules 2.01, 9.14 and 9.15) – Disseminated and discussed a sexual
assault report with his coworkers, causing a coworker to be embarrassed and uncomfortable
within his place of employment (General Police Orders 1.1.39 and 7.03.02 and Manual of Rules
2.01, 9.14 and 9.15) – Pled guilty to a Misdemeanor of the First Degree for violating Ohio
Revised Code 2913.049(c) Attempted Unauthorized use of property; computer; cable; or
telecommunication property or service (Manual of Rules 5.01) – Placed in LEADSDENY in
accordance with the LEADS Security Policy (General Police Order 7.02.04).

Disposition: Found “Guilty” of two Group II violations and three Group III violations.
Considering the member’s active discipline history (6/2/2022: twenty-day suspension,
10/23/2023: ten-day suspension), the member was terminated from employment with the City of
Cleveland, Department of Public Safety, Division of Police effective March 6, 2024. This
corrective action is within the Group III violation range of the discipline matrix when
aggravating factors (multiple violations arising from a single event or incident may escalate the
disciplinary action to the maximum group range, active discipline) outweigh mitigating factors
(none).

Rationale: On two occasions, the officer improperly accessed a Law Enforcement Database for
purposes outside of his official duties. This was evident by his initial “Guilty Plea” to a
Misdemeanor of the First Degree violation of Ohio Revised Code 2913.04 (c). As a result of his
conduct, the officer was placed into a “LEADS Deny” status. As such, he is no longer able to
perform his duties as a Law Enforcement Officer. In addition, the officer discussed the contents
of this unauthorized information with other members of his unit and caused another member to
be embarrassed. This created an uncomfortable work environment within their place of
employment.
Charges: Database Misuse (LERMS/LEADS/OHLEG),Arrest / Criminal Charge,Confidential Information Violation

Charge & Discipline Decision:
Specifications: Conduct that would constitute the crime of possession of firearm in beer liquor
permit premises (Manual of Rules 2.01) – Diminished the esteem (×2) (Manual of Rules 5.01
and 5.04) – Arrested for Operating Vehicle While Under the Influence of Alcohol or Drugs
(OVI) (M-1) (Ohio Revised Code Section 4511.19 & Manual of Rules 2.01) – Arrested for
Improper Handling of Firearms in a Motor Vehicle (F-5) (Ohio Revised Code Section 2923.15
and Manual of Rules 2.01) – Arrested for Speeding (MM) (Ohio Revised Code Section 4511.21
and Manual of Rules 2.01).
Disposition: Found “Guilty” of four Group II violations and two Group III violations.
Considering the member’s active discipline history (8/10/2022: 3-day suspension), the member
was terminated from employment with the City of Cleveland, Department of Public Safety,
Division of Police, effective July 3, 2024. This corrective action is within the First Group III
Violation range of the discipline matrix when aggravating factors (active discipline, multiple
violations arising from a single event or incident may escalate the disciplinary action to the
maximum group range, criminal conviction where the offense had the potential to cause serious
harm to the public, diminished the esteem of the Division) outweigh mitigating factors (accepted
responsibility).
Rationale: The member completed his shift working part-time at The Ivy (1212 West 6th Street).
After the member completed his shift he consumed five servings of Buffalo Trace Bourbon,
while at The Ivy, in the Cleveland Division of Police uniform, and while in the presence of
employees of The Ivy. As The Ivy is a beer and liquor permit premises, the consumption while
armed is a violation of Ohio Revised Code §2923.121. The member then chose to drive away
and was observed driving at least 93 miles per hour by the Ohio State Highway Patrol. He was
pulled over for the traffic violation and was determined to be operating his vehicle while under
the influence of alcohol (found guilty in Avon Lake Municipal Court: Ohio Revised Code
§4511.19). Because he was still armed, he was also in violation of Improper Handling of
Firearms in a Motor Vehicle – Ohio Revised Code §2923.16, which he later pled down to Using
Weapons While Intoxicated – Ohio Revised Code §2923.15. Additionally, his actions garnered
local media attention, as this story was covered by Channel 3 news, Channel 8 news, and
Channel 19 news. Multiple violations arising from a single event or incident may escalate the
disciplinary action to the maximum group range.

Specifications: Possession of a firearm while intoxicated (Ohio Revised Code 2923.15 and
Manual of Rules 2.01) – Consumed alcoholic beverages inside of a beer/liquor premises while in
possession of a firearm (Ohio Revised Code 2923.121 and Manual of Rules 2.01) – Sick leave
abuse in the Third Quarter of 2024 (General Police Order 1.3.06) – Arrested for Operating
Vehicle While Under the Influence of Alcohol or Drugs (OVI) (M-1) and for Operating a
Vehicle Under the Influence of Alcohol violator within twenty years of previously having been
convicted of or pleaded guilty to Refuse to Test (Ohio Revised Code Section 4511.19 and
Manual of Rules 2.01).

Disposition: Found “Guilty” of one Group I violation, one Group II violation and two Group III
violations. Considering the member’s active discipline history (none), the member was
terminated from employment with the City of Cleveland, Department of Public Safety, Division
of Police effective September 12, 2025. This corrective action is within the First Group III
Violation range of the discipline matrix when aggravating factors (multiple violations arising
from a single event or incident may escalate the disciplinary action to the maximum group
range, criminal conviction where the offense had the potential to cause serious harm to the
public, failure to successfully begin a rehabilitation program) outweigh mitigating factors
(accepted responsibility, no active discipline).

Rationale: On August 30, 2024, while off-duty, the member consumed alcoholic beverages
inside of a beer/liquor premises while in possession of a firearm (loaded and had a magazine
with six live rounds). That evening he also had the firearm in public while intoxicated. On
January 17, 2025, the member was arrested by the Cleveland Metroparks Police Department for
Ohio Revised Code §4511.19(A)(1)(a) – Operating Vehicle Under the Influence of Alcohol or
Drugs (OVI) and Ohio Revised Code §4511.19(A)(2)(b) – Operating a Vehicle Under the
Influence of Alcohol violator within twenty years of previously having been convicted of or
pleaded guilty to Refuse to Test. On May 21, 2025, he pled guilty to Ohio Revised Code
§4511.19(A)(1)(a) – Operating Vehicle Under the Influence of Alcohol or Drugs (OVI) in
Cleveland Municipal court. This was the member’s second OVI conviction. Additionally, the
member exhibited a pattern of sick leave abuse, to wit: during the Third Quarter of 2024, he used
60 hours of undocumented sick leave. Multiple violations arising from a single event or incident
may escalate the disciplinary action to the maximum group range.
Charges: Improper Handling of Firearm,Arrest / Criminal Charge

Charge & Discipline Decision:
Specifications: Engaged in a verbal argument with another member of the Cleveland Division of
Police and assaulted him, resulting in an injury to the other member’s finger (City of Cleveland –
Workplace Violence Policy, General Police Order 1.1.08 and Manual of Rules 2.01) –
Untruthful during Garrity interview (Manual of Rules 3.12) – Discourteous and/or disrespectful
during Garrity interview (Manual of Rules 5.09).

Disposition: Found “Guilty” of one Group I violation and two Group III violations. Considering
the member’s active discipline history (11/18/2022: 8-day suspension), the member was
terminated from employment with the City of Cleveland, Department of Public Safety, Division
of Police effective September 16, 2025. This corrective action is within the First Group III
Violation range of the discipline matrix when aggravating factors (active discipline, multiple
violations arising from a single event or incident may escalate the disciplinary action to the
maximum group range, dishonesty creates a presumption of termination) outweigh mitigating
factors (none).

Rationale: On December 14, 2024, while inside of the Second District Headquarters, the
member engaged in an argument with another officer of the Division of Police. During the
course of this incident, the member approached the officer and slapped him in the face, which
was witnessed by a supervisor. In response, the other officer pushed the member away to create
physical distance between them, and in doing so, he fractured the top part of his right-hand ring
finger. On April 3, 2025, the member was untruthful with Internal Affairs Unit investigators
during a Garrity interview by stating, “No,” denying that he struck and/or slapped the face of
another member of the Cleveland Division of Police during the physical altercation inside the
Second District Headquarters (dishonesty carries a presumption of termination). During that
interview, he referred to other members of the Division of Police disparagingly. Multiple
violations arising from a single event or incident may escalate the disciplinary action to the
maximum group range.

Specifications: Indicted for Forgery and pled guilty to Attempted Forgery (Ohio Revised Code
2923.02/2913.31 and Manual of Rules 2.01) – Submitted two forged medical documents
(General Police Order 1.07.06 and Manual of Rules 2.01) – Step-1 Sick Abuse violation
(General Police Order 1.3.06) – Absent Without Leave (AWOL) (Manual of Rules 10.01).
Disposition: Found “Guilty” of two Group I violations, one Group II violation and one Group III
violation. Considering the member’s active discipline history (6/27/2024: 2-day suspension), the
member was terminated from employment with the City of Cleveland, Department of Public
Safety, Division of Police effective January 13, 2026. This corrective action is within the First
Group III Violation range of the discipline matrix when aggravating factors (multiple violations
arising from a single event or incident may escalate the disciplinary action to the maximum
group range, active discipline) outweigh mitigating factors (accepted responsibility).
Rationale: To avoid progression in the Sick Abuse Control Program, on July 31, 2024, the
member submitted two forged documents covering sick leave taken on May 16, 2024, and July
26, 2024. Subsequently, she was indicted under Ohio Revised Code §2913.31(A)(1) – Forgery
and was found “Guilty” of an amended charge of Ohio Revised Code §2923.02/§2913.31 A (1) –
Attempted Forgery. As such, the member did not submit acceptable documentation for sick leave
taken on May 16, 2024, and July 26, 2024 while on Step-1 of the Sick Leave Abuse Control
Program. Additionally, on June 4, 2025, the member was Absent With Out Leave (AWOL) when
she arrived at 0708 hours, 38 minutes late, for her 0630 shift. Multiple violations arising from a
single event or incident may escalate the disciplinary action to the maximum group range.
Charges: Absent Without Leave (AWOL),Sick Leave Abuse,Untruthfulness,Arrest / Criminal Charge

Charge & Discipline Decision:
Specification: Failed to investigate a crime after receiving information that an aggravated robbery had taken place and failed to secure the crime scene, allowing a person involved to remove items from an involved vehicle (Manual of Rules 4. OJ) - Failed to properly complete an OH-1 Hit-Skip Investigation after the officer received information on-scene the operator of one of the vehicles was present and was shown a video of the crash and failed to include this information in the investigation ( General Police Order 8.1. 04) - Failed to properly investigate and document an incident, this conduct would tend to reasonably diminish the esteem of the Cleveland Division of Police (CDP) in the eyes of the public (Manual of Rules 5. 01) - Untruthful when the officer completed an OH-1 report that stated, "While on scene there was nobody on-scene and nobody was in the vehicle." The driver involved in the crash was on-scene and multiple citizens advised the officer that the person was the driver of one of the vehicles involved in the crash (Manual of Rules 3.12) - Failed to activate the Wearable Camera System (WCS) when interacting with a driver involved in the Motor Vehicle Crash (General Police Order 4. 06. 04) - Charged in Cleveland Municipal Court with the following Ohio Revised Codes: 2921.45 Interfering with Civil Rights (M-1), 2921.13 Falsification (M-1), 2921.31 Obstructing Official Business (M-2) and 2921.44 Dereliction of Duty (M2) (Manual of Rules 2.01). 

Disposition: Found "Not Guilty" of being charged in Cleveland Municipal Court with Ohio Revised Codes: 2921.45 Interfering with Civil Rights (M-1), 2921.13 Falsification (M-1), 2921.31 Obstructing Official Business (M-2) and 2921.44 Dereliction of Duty (M2) after the charges were dismissed after a defense motion for Rule 29 was granted. Found "Guilty" of one Group I violation, three Group II violations and one Group III violation. On January 6, 2023, the officer was terminated from employment with the City of Cleveland, Department of Public Safety, Division of Police. This discipline is within the First Group III violation range of the discipline matrix when aggravating factors (multiple violations arising from a single event or incident may escalate the disciplinary action to the maximum group range, actions caused serious injury or harm to the public) outweigh mitigating factors (no active discipline). 

Rationale: The officer displayed conduct that would tend to diminish the esteem of the Division of Police and exhibited a complete disregard for ethical behavior and significantly compromised the reputation of the Division and its mission. The officer failed to properly complete an OH-1 Hit-Skip investigation after receiving information that the operator of one of the vehicles was on­scene and returned to the scene on three separate occasions while the officer was present. The officer failed to properly complete an OH-1 Hit-Skip investigation, failed to investigate an aggravated robbery that had taken place, and failed to secure the crime scene. After being shown a video of the crash by a resident whose parked car was struck, the officer failed to include the information/evidence in the investigation, this conduct would tend to reasonably diminish the esteem of the Division of Police. While completing the OH-1 crash report, the officer was untruthful when the report stated that the hit-skip driver was not on-scene. WCS recordings clearly show that the involved female was present while the officer was on-scene at least three separate times and that one of the victims provided the officer with video evidence of the crash. The officer failed to activate the Wearable Camera System when interacting with a driver involved in a motor vehicle crash. In addition, the officer was charged in Cleveland Municipal Court with Ohio Revised Codes: 2921.45 Interfering with Civil Rights (M-1), 2921.13  Falsification (M-1), 2921.31 Obstructing Official Business (M-2) and 2921.44 Dereliction of Duty (M2); however, those charges were dismissed after a defense motion for Rule 29 was grant
Charges: WCS Violation,Lack of Service,Conduct Unbecoming / Diminished Esteem,Untruthfulness,Ethics Violation

Charge & Discipline Decision:
Specification: Unprofessional conduct (discourteous comments) (General Police Order 1.1.02,
and Manual of Rules 5.01, 5.02 and 5.09) – Failed to investigate (Manual of Rules 4.01) –
Improper search (General Police Order 2.02.02) – Improper stop (unreasonable amount of time)
(General Police Order 2.02.01) – Failed to request medical attention (Manual of Rules 4.14) –
Discourteous (Manual of Rules 4.14) – Sick abuse (undesirable duty) (General Police Order
1.3.06) – Unlawful Restraint (Ohio Revised Code 2905.03 and Manual of Rules 2.01) – Failed to
investigate (Operating a Vehicle while Impaired offense) (General Police Order 8.2.05 and
Manual of Rules 4.01) – Allegation of Domestic Violence (Ohio Revised Code 2919.25 and
Manual of Rules 2.01) – Allegation of Telecommunications Harassment (Ohio Revised Code
2917.21 and Manual of Rules 2.01) – Untruthful in his Garrity interview (×3) (Manual of Rules
3.12).

Disposition: The officer was found “Not Guilty” of the charges of sick abuse, failing to
investigate an “operating a vehicle while impaired” offense, engaging in conduct that would
constitute the crime of domestic violence, engaging in conduct that would constitute the crime of
telecommunications harassment and the three untruthful charges, as such, the charges were
dismissed. Found “Guilty” of one Group I violation, five Group II violations and one Group III
violation. Considering the member’s active discipline history (9/10/2021: seven-day suspension,
12/9/2021: eight-day suspension, 3/18/2022: twenty-day suspension, 10/31/2022: thirty-day
suspension), the member was terminated from employment with the City of Cleveland,
Department of Public Safety, Division of Police effective Monday, November 20, 2023. This
corrective action is within the Group III violation range of the discipline matrix when
aggravating factors (multiple violations arising from a single event or incident may escalate the
disciplinary action to the maximum group range, active discipline) outweigh mitigating factors
(none).

Rationale: The officer provided documentation for the sick abuse, left the scene of an “operating
a vehicle while impaired” investigation to assist with a high priority assignment and only after
the driver had been taken to the hospital, did not commit the allegation of Domestic Violence or
Telecommunications Harassment, and was not untruthful during his Garrity interviews. The
officer was unprofessional and discourteous in his comments to a complainant, failed to
investigate two separate assault allegations when he released an alleged suspect prior to properly
gathering information from both victims and failed to identify the suspect, and conducted an
unreasonable search by going into a detained male’s pockets to retrieve a wallet without having
consent to do so or having an exception to the search without a warrant rule. In addition, the
officer did not limit the detention of an individual to a reasonable amount of time, failed to
request medical attention for an assault victim, was discourteous to an assault victim, and did
engage in conduct that would constitute the crime of Unlawful Restraint (Ohio Revised Code
2905.03) when he physically prevented a female from leaving her residence.
Charges: Sick Leave Abuse,Improper Search/Frisk,Lack of Service,Use of Force Violation,Failed to Request Medical Attention,Unprofessional Conduct,Offensive Remarks,Improper Stop,Telecommunications Violation

While assigned as the missing persons car, did fail to tour areas of interest for an
endangered missing juvenile (Manual of Rules 2.14 and 4.18) – While assigned as the missing
persons car, did report that the area was toured in contradiction of assigned patrol cars’
Automatic Vehicle Locators (AVL) (Manual of Rules 3.12) – While assigned as the missing
persons car, did spend eight (8) hours, nine (9) hours, five (5) hours, six (6) hours and four (4)
hours during different tours of duty at the District Headquarters, as indicated by the assigned
patrol cars’ Automatic Vehicle Locators (AVL) (Manual of Rules 4.06). The officer was
assigned as a one-man car and detailed as the missing persons car. The officer carried out the
responsibilities and followed the corresponding General Police Orders. The Wearable Camera
System videos show that the officer handled the assignments, required follow-ups, assisted on
other assignments and provided professional service to all citizens during each contact made.
The reports that the officer completed were all reviewed and signed by a superior officer within
the District without any corrections. If there was an issue with the language of the reports or the
patrol car being stationary in the district parking lot, it should have been immediately addressed

by the superior officer responsible for reviewing and approving the reports or the Officer-in-
Charge of the shift responsible for monitoring the AVL system. Found “Not Guilty” and the

disciplinary action was dismissed.
Charges: Lack of Service,Neglect of Duty,Travel Violation

Charge & Discipline Decision:
Patrol Officer: Conducted a strip search of the complainant in an area that did not provide
privacy, outside, in a residential area instead of a holding facility, and failed to take appropriate
steps to minimize the complainant’s embarrassment and ensure the correct number of personnel
were present while searching the complainant’s private areas (General Police Order 2.02.04 and
Manual of Rules 2.03, 2.04 and 4.03). A thorough review of Sections I.A. and III.A. of General
Police Order 2.02.04 revealed that the officer adhered to policy. Further, the officer was
following orders from an immediate supervisor to conduct the strip search, conducted the search
quickly and efficiently, and the search resulted in the recovery of a suspected bag of narcotics
without incident. Found “Not Guilty” and the disciplinary action was dismissed.

Authorized a strip search of the complainant outside the confines of a secure
holding facility (General Police Order 2.02.04 and Manual of Rules 2.03, 2.04 and 4.03). A
thorough review of Sections I.A. and III.A. of General Police Order 2.02.04 revealed that the
officer adhered to policy. Prior to authorizing the strip search, a scale with suspected narcotics
residue, plastic baggies and disposable gloves were observed in plain view inside of the vehicle.
While conducting a Terry pat down the officer felt what he determined through his training and
experience to be a bag of suspected narcotics in the complainants “crotch area.” The complainant
was provided multiple opportunities to voluntarily hand over the suspected narcotics to which he
refused and attempted to flee. Additionally, exposure to trace amounts of fentanyl can cause an
overdose or death, giving clear exigent circumstances to authorize the strip search on-scene for
the safety of the complainant. The search was conducted quickly and efficiently, and the search resulted in the recovery of a suspected bag of narcotics without incident. Found “Not Guilty” and
the disciplinary action was dismissed.

Hearing Held By: Acting Deputy Chief of Field Operations
Charge & Discipline Decision:
Referred to a complainant as “Mr. Don’t Touch Me” during an interaction, and
the statements were mocking in nature (General Police Order 2.01.02 and Manual of Rules 5.01,
5.08, and 5.09). The officer's actions and language were used to de-escalate the situation, and the
language, while seemingly unorthodox, was not "mocking in nature." The officer did begin using
the complainant’s real name, and the complainant reacted negatively, so the officer reverted to
"Mr. Don't Touch Me." Found "Not Guilty," and the disciplinary action was dismissed.

Specification: Failed to ask any substantive questions and left important decisions to subordinate
officers, causing a delay in the complainant’s medical care (General Police Order 3.02.01 and
Manual of Rules 1.06).
Disposition: Found “Not Guilty” and the disciplinary action was dismissed.
Rationale: There were no indications that the complainant needed medical attention until
statements were made that he was having “heart palpitations” when the complainant arrived at
the jail.

Specification: Between February 1, 2022, and June 30, 2022, the officer displayed a pattern of
sick leave abuse by using two hundred-eight hours of sick leave on weekends or on days that
precede or follow scheduled vacation days or compensatory time (General Police Order 1.3.06).
Disposition: Found “Not Guilty” and the disciplinary action was dismissed.
Rationale: Documentation was provided before the hearing excusing the officer’s absence.

Hearing Held By: "Assistant Director of Public Safety, Administrative Operations"
Charge & Discipline Decision:
Specifications: Used undocumented sick leave and failed to provide documentation to the
Medical Director while on Step-1 of the Sick Leave Abuse Control Program (General Police
Order 1.3.06 and Manual of Rules 10.01 and 10.02) – Failed to report for duty at the assigned
time without notifying a supervisor (General Police Order 1.3.24 and Manual of Rules 10.01) –
Was aware of a complainant’s need for immediate medical attention, and transported the
complainant to jail instead of the hospital (General Police Order 3.02.01) – Failed to provide the
complainant with his name and badge number after repeatedly being asked (Manual of Rules
4.13).
Disposition: Found “Not Guilty” and the disciplinary action was dismissed.
Rationale: The sick abuse was previously addressed in a pre-disciplinary hearing and found “not
guilty” in a prior disposition letter. The officer provided documentation to the Medical Unit and
was returned to work in accordance with the policy regarding the failure to report for duty. There
were no indications that the complainant needed medical attention until statements were made
that he was having “heart palpitations” when the complainant arrived at the jail. During this call
for service, Wearable Camera System (WCS) video shows that the officer did provide his name
and badge number to the arrested male multiple times.
Charges: Sick Leave Abuse,Failure to Report/Notify,Failed to Request Medical Attention,Failed to Identify

Hearing Held By: "Assistant Director of Public Safety, Administrative Operations"
Charge & Discipline Decision:
Specification: Aware of a complainant’s need for immediate medical attention, and transported

Specification: Diminished the esteem of the Division of Police when a victim could be heard

Hearing Held By: "Assistant Director of Public Safety, Administrative Operations"
Charge & Discipline Decision:
Specification: Improperly searched a complainant and a vehicle without permission and without reasonable suspicion (General Police Order 2.02.02). 
Disposition: Found "Not Guilty" and the disciplinary action was dismissed. 
Rationale: The stop and search of the vehicle was lawful based on the totality of the circumstances (knowledge of the complainant's history with narcotics, picking up a female with a prior history of narcotics and prostitution, and the vehicle in question was impeding the flow of traffic) and the female passenger in the vehicle admitted to having heroin on her person prior to the searching of the vehicle.

Failed to place the Wearable camera System (WCS) into event mode prior to  responding to an assignment to assist members waiting on a tow of two motor vehicles (General  Police Order 4.06.04). 

Found “Not Guilty” and the disciplinary action was dismissed. 

Because the officer was just completing a vehicle tow for another officer and he had  no contact with the public, the officer did not violate General Police Order 4.06.04 as currently  written.

Specification: Lacked the required reasonable suspicion to conduct a frisk search of a male and
authorized a search of the male’s vehicle, absent the probable cause to do so (General Police
Order 2.02.02).

Disposition: Found “Not Guilty” and the disciplinary action was dismissed.

Rationale: The actions of the individual, beginning with the “slow roll” and continuing with the
individual moving a bag away from himself and subsequently lying about moving the bag were
all actions that would be considered danger cues and would alert an officer to heightened
awareness for their safety. The officer had prior knowledge of this individual being involved in
drug trafficking on numerous occasions, and has extensive training and experience on criminal
indicators on traffic stops and search and seizure. The officer had reasonable suspicion to
conduct a lunge area check and a pat-down of the front seat passenger for weapons. Upon
finding marijuana in the lunge area, probable cause to search the vehicle existed.

Specification: Lacked the required reasonable suspicion not only to conduct a frisk search of a
male, but a protective search of a male’s bag.

Disposition: Found “Not Guilty” and the disciplinary action was dismissed.

Rationale: The actions of the individual, beginning with the “slow roll” and continuing with the
individual moving a bag away from himself and subsequently lying about moving the bag were
all actions which would be considered danger cues and would alert an officer to heightened
awareness for their safety. The officer also noted that the individual looked nervous, and the
officer had prior knowledge of this individual being involved in drug trafficking on numerous
occasions. The officer has had extensive training and experience on criminal indicators on traffic
stops and search and seizure. The officer had reasonable suspicion to conduct a frisk search on
the individual and a protective search of the individual’s bag.

Deployed his City issued Conducted Electrical Weapon (CEW) at a suspect that  did not pose a threat of physical harm to the officer, bystanders or themselves (General Police  Order 2.01.04). 

Found “Not Guilty” and the disciplinary action was dismissed. 

The officer was responding to a call for a crime of violence and engaged in a foot  pursuit, during which the officer shouted several warnings on the use of the taser. The officer did not deploy the taser, recognizing that tasing a fleeing subject would likely be prohibited by  policy. The officer was not aware of the location of assisting officers and after coming to a fence,  he gave additional warnings and intended to continue pursuit of the suspect. The officer described the suspect as getting up off the ground and anticipating a fight. The WCS was  dislodged during the pursuit, therefore unable to provide additional facts to support any other  finding.
Charges: Improper Handling of Firearm,Use of Force Violation

Charge & Discipline Decision:
Specification: Failed to enforce the Divisions Domestic Violence Statutes by not arresting a
suspect, by allowing a suspect to leave the immediate area, by not taking any photographs of
alleged injuries or damage done to the residence, by not requesting a supervisor when the
primary physical aggressor could not be identified, by not requesting a victim complete and sign
a Misdemeanor Complaint Statement and by not providing a victim with Domestic Violence
paperwork (General Police Order 5.05.01 and Manual of Rules 2.02 and 2.03).

Disposition: Found “Not Guilty” and the disciplinary action was dismissed.

Rationale: The officer was a Probationary Patrol Officer (PPO) during the assignment and was
outside detaining a possible suspect while their Field Training Officer (FTO) was inside the
residence with a victim. The officer was detaining a possible suspect and was not aware of the
victim’s condition or statements, nor the condition of the inside of the house, and the information
was not relayed to him.
Charges: Lack of Service,Neglect of Duty,Evidence Collection Violation

Charge & Discipline Decision:
Specification: Failed to investigate other parties involved in an accident (General Police Order
8.01.02).

Disposition: Found “Not Guilty” and the disciplinary action was dismissed.

Rationale: While on the scene of an accident, the officer ensured a vehicle was moved out of
traffic and positioned the patrol car to cordon off the area for the investigation. The officer then
assisted in getting the license plate number of a vehicle and when he returned, the other driver
had already fled on foot. Because he was unaware of any possible criminal activity, it would
have been inappropriate to detain anyone.

Specification: Failed to generate a report and make an arrest for domestic violence despite
evidence that a third party witness observed an assault of a family member (Manual of Rules
4.18, 9.01 and 9.07).

Disposition: Found “Not Guilty” and the disciplinary action was dismissed.

Rationale: There was a lack of evidence due to the delay in reporting the allegation. The
incident took place on December 19, 2019, but was not reported until April 13, 2022. As such,
this delay prevented obtaining Wearable Camera System video of the incident. In addition, the
member’s Collective Bargaining Agreement prohibits discipline when a citizen complaint is filed
more than six months after the date of the alleged event.

Specification: Failed to generate a report and make an arrest for domestic violence despite
evidence that a third party witness observed an assault of a family member (Manual of Rules
4.18, 9.01 and 9.07).

Disposition: Found “Not Guilty” and the disciplinary action was dismissed.

Rationale: There was a lack of evidence due to the delay in reporting the allegation. The
incident took place on December 19, 2019, but was not reported until April 13, 2022. As such,
this delay prevented obtaining Wearable Camera System video of the incident. In addition, the
member’s Collective Bargaining Agreement prohibits discipline when a citizen complaint is filed
more than six months after the date of the alleged event.

Specification: The officer hit the complainant’s window, accused him of having drugs in his
vehicle, snatched his door open, and was extremely hostile when he identified something that the
officer thought might have been contraband (Manual of Rules 5.01, 5.08 and 5.09).

Disposition: Found “Not Guilty” and the disciplinary action was dismissed.

Rationale: The officer had a legal reason for the stop (window tint) which also hindered his
ability to see clearly into the vehicle. Had the driver opened the window more, this may have
allowed the officer to better see the baggie with coins in it that he mistook for a baggie filled
with marijuana. Because the baggie was in plain view, the officer had probable cause to
investigate further, and because the complainant would not lower the window any further or
open the door, the officer opened the car door to further investigate the baggie. The conduct
during the stop is not in violation of any policies or directives of the Cleveland Division of
Police and the rationale regarding dark window tint laws includes officer safety, as the ability for
officers to see threats is diminished.

Specification: Failed to use de-escalation tactics and training (General Police Order 2.01.02 and
Manual of Rules 5.01, 5.08 and 5.09).

Disposition: Found “Not Guilty” and the disciplinary action was dismissed.

Rationale: The officer responded to an assignment of a violent assault of an Emergency Medical
Services worker and upon exiting the patrol car and first engaging the suspect he yelled for the
suspect to get on the “fucking” ground. Upon observing that the suspect was no longer

aggressive and was now only passively resisting (by not getting on the ground), the officer self-
deescalated by continuing to give firm orders, without cursing again. In addition, the member’s

Collective Bargaining Agreement prohibits discipline when a citizen complaint is filed more than
six months after the date of the alleged event.

Specification: Failed to follow specific interviewing steps and obtain a statement from the
suspect (Detective Unit Manual – Detective Responsibilities).

Disposition: Found “Not Guilty” and the disciplinary action was dismissed.

Rationale: The officer did make attempts to contact the suspect by looking up reports in Field
Based Reporting to find an address or phone number for the complainant, but there was nothing
of value in the reports and found that the complainant is transient and sporadically stays at 2100
Lakeside Avenue. The officer followed up on this investigation, including attempts to find a way
to contact the complainant for an interview, and the complainant, including his history with the
Cleveland Division of Police, lacked a viable method to contact him. In addition, the member’s
Collective Bargaining Agreement prohibits discipline when a citizen complaint is filed more than
six months after the date of the alleged event.

Specification: Failed to notify her direct supervisor that a prosecutor was unavailable for a
criminality review after being instructed to formally charge a suspect with the misdemeanor
charge of Endangering Children (Detective Unit Manual and Manual of Rules 4.03) – Failed to
obtain the required prosecutor’s signature on a Misdemeanor Complaint Affidavit and instead
typed the prosecutor’s name on the Affidavit. The officer submitted the paperwork to the Clerk’s
Office to hold an arrested suspect in jail without authorization from the prosecutor (Detective
Unit Manual and Manual of Rules 4.17) – Failed to follow the complete directions given to her
by a City of Cleveland prosecutor. The prosecutor held felony criminal charges in abeyance for an arrested suspect and instructed the officer to conduct further investigation by contacting the
Department of Child and Family Services and the Fire Arson Unit. Despite obtaining enough
information to file misdemeanor charges to hold the suspect in jail, she failed to follow through
with further investigation as instructed (Manual of Rules 4.18).

Disposition: Found “Not Guilty” and the disciplinary action was dismissed.

Rationale: The decision to charge the suspect was based upon the confession of the suspect and
at the direction of her immediate supervisor, who was aware that a prosecutor was not available
for criminal review. The situation was further exacerbated, as the Cleveland Division of Police
then lacked a contingency plan when a prosecutor was not available and timely charging is
crucial. Additionally, neither the Fire Arson Unit nor the Division of Children and Family
Services were available during this critical time.

Specification: Refused to work mandatory overtime after being ordered to do so (Manual of
Rules 2.02, 2.04, 2.06 and 2.07).

Disposition: Found “Not Guilty” and the disciplinary action was dismissed.

Rationale: This is the officer’s only instance of using sick leave when she was to be mandated
for overtime and she provided satisfactory documentation regarding the sick leave taken.

Specification: Failed to de-escalate (General Police Order 5.11.03 and 2.01.02) – Told a
complainant’s neighbor that she had responded to a “psych incident” (Manual of Rules 5.01) –
Failed to ensure that the required documentation for emergency hospitalization had been
completed and submitted upon a Complainant’s transport to the hospital (General Police Order
5.11.03).

Disposition: Found “Not Guilty” and the disciplinary action was dismissed.

Rationale: The officer responded to an assignment regarding the complainant being possibly
suicidal. The officer interviewing the neighbor as part of the investigation asked about what had
happened and the officer used generalities to describe the incident. The required form(s) were
completed and submitted for transport to and acceptance by the hospital. Any form required by
General Police Order is superseded by any form required by hospitals for patient admission and
had the required forms not been completed, the hospital would not have accepted the
complainant. In addition, the complaint was filed beyond the six-month time period in which the
incident occurred; the member’s Collective Bargaining Agreement prohibits discipline when a
citizen complaint is filed more than six months after the date of the alleged event.
Charges: Failed to De-escalate,Neglect of Duty,Offensive Remarks

Charge & Discipline Decision:
Specification: Failed to utilize de-escalation tactics when responding to a call for a crisis
intervention (General Police Orders 5.11.03 and 2.01.02) – Made comments about a
complainant’s sexual orientation that were not useful or beneficial for delivery of police services
(Manual of Rules 5.11).

Disposition: Found “Not Guilty” and the disciplinary action was dismissed.

Rationale: The complaint was filed beyond the six-month time period in which the incident
occurred, the member’s Collective Bargaining Agreement prohibits discipline when a citizen
complaint is filed more than six months after the date of the alleged event.

Specification: Failed to properly categorize the appropriate level of force and record it as such in
Division tracking software (General Police Order 2.01.03) – Used an unnecessary level of force
by placing his knee on the head of the suspect when a reasonably effective alternative to the use
of force existed (e.g. using hands to subdue the suspect’s head instead of a knee) (General Police
Order 2.01.03).

Disposition: Found “Not Guilty” and the disciplinary action was dismissed.

Rationale: The investigating officers acknowledge that the officer’s account of the use of force
was both accurate and factual. The officer’s description of the force used was approved by his
chain of command. A review of the lobby camera footage shows that his use of force was
calculated and without malicious intent as he was helping other officers in securing a combative
citizen and his use of force lasted approximately twenty seconds and ended when officers were
able to successfully secure the citizen.
Charges: Use of Force Violation,Failure to Report/Intervene (UOF)

Charge & Discipline Decision:
Specification: Failed to identify as a Police Officer when taking a police action off-duty and in
plain clothes (General Police Order 3.4.23).
Disposition: Found “Not Guilty” and the disciplinary action was dismissed.
Rationale: The officers’ actions did not constitute a police action when he initially approached
the complainant and did leave the area but was followed by the complainant.

Specification: Failed to apprise his commanding officer of possible mandated staffing changes
required of the Cleveland Division of Police at Cleveland Hopkins International Airport
(General Police Order 4.05.02).
Disposition: Found “Not Guilty” and the disciplinary action was dismissed.
Rationale: Testimony revealed a phone call the officer had with his then Deputy Chief where he
was informed of the possible changes, and interviews of the officer’s direct supervisor or his
Commander were not completed.

Specification: Discourteousness in speech (Manual of Rules 5.09).
Disposition: Found “Not Guilty” and the disciplinary action was dismissed.
Rationale: The officer was off-duty and he did not indicate that he was a member of the
Cleveland Division of Police nor did any attire represent him as a member of the Cleveland
Division of Police while he was engaged in a private argument with another person.

Specification: Failed to notify Communication Control Section (CCS) that a pursuit was
underway (General Police Order 3.2.02 and Divisional Notice #16-304).
Disposition: Found “Not Guilty” and the disciplinary action was dismissed.
Rationale: The officer engaged in a pursuit and was broadcasting information regarding the
suspect vehicle’s location and direction. Once he engaged with the suspect vehicle, there was
significant radio traffic which appeared to hinder his ability to broadcast that he was now in a
pursuit which lasted about 20 seconds, as such the officer had limited opportunity to notify the
Communications Control Section (CCS) that he was in a pursuit.

Specification: Failed to have a supervisor present during his interview with a complainant’s
juvenile son (CDP Detective Unit Manual – Procedures of Juvenile Offenders).

Disposition: Found “Not Guilty” and the disciplinary action was dismissed.

Rationale: The juvenile’s mother and adult sister were present and the language in the Cleveland
Division of Police Detective Unit Manual would indicate to a detective that a supervisor is not
required when obtaining a statement from a juvenile when a parent or guardian is present.

Specification: Failed to actively investigate a threat made against a complainant (Manual of
Rules 4.01).

Disposition: Found “Not Guilty” and the disciplinary action was dismissed.

Rationale: The complainant was not aware of the threat and he could not be a victim of
Aggravated Menacing. In addition, the complaint was filed beyond the six-month time period in
which the incident occurred; the member’s Collective Bargaining Agreement prohibits discipline
when a citizen complaint is filed more than six months after the date of the alleged event.

Specification: Issued a Miranda warning to a juvenile and interviewed the juvenile without the presence of a supervisor (General Police Order 2.02.03 and Cleveland Division of Police Detective Unit Manual 4.19). 

Disposition: Found “Not Guilty” and the disciplinary action was dismissed. 

Rationale: A superior officer/supervisor did respond to the location and was present during the interaction with the juvenile offender. The Office of Professional Standards acknowledged that the role or responsibilities of the supervisor, when on-scene, is not clear and acknowledged that it is not well defined as to what proximity the supervisor should be to the participants, when the interview is being conducted. The Office of Professional Standards later acknowledged that the presence of another senior detective on-scene, did meet the requirements.

Specification: Caused serious physical harm to a female by shoving her down stairs, causing an
incapacitating arm injury (Manual of Rules 2.01) – Made threats to damage property of his
girlfriend by threatening to break down her door (Manual of Rules 2.01).

Disposition: Found “Not Guilty” and the disciplinary action was dismissed.

Rationale: The evidence collected and presented at the hearing was comprised of second and
third-hand statements of a family member and co-worker of the victim. The victim, who was
present at the hearing, was adamant that the accusations revealed by the Internal Affairs
investigation were either not accurate or false. She also maintained that the officer had not
subjected her to any physical harm and her testimony directly refuted the allegations made
against the officer.
Charges: Use of Force Violation,Arrest / Criminal Charge

Charge & Discipline Decision:
Specification: Untruthful (Manual of Rules 3.12).

Disposition: The disciplinary action was dismissed.

Rationale: The member was not afforded the “Notice of Investigation” as required by the
CPPA–Civilian Personnel, Article 31. The application of due process involves not only the
investigation, but also the manner in which all required obligations and timelines are met. This
responsibility require great attention. Without proper due process, discipline cannot be issued.

Specification: Sick leave abuse in the Second Quarter of 2023 (General Police Order 1.3.06).

Disposition: Found “Not Guilty” and the disciplinary action was dismissed.

Rationale: The officer had provided documentation explaining his sick leave.

Specifications: Failed to restrict overtime to the minimum when he created three hours of
overtime by adjusting his start times (General Police Order 4.05.02) – Failed to restrict overtime
to the minimum when he submitted overlapping overtime cards, causing double overtime
payments (pyramiding) to occur for combined total of five hours (General Police Order
4.05.02).
Disposition: As a result of a Settlement Agreement between the City of Cleveland, the member
and the member’s Union, the two-day suspension is rescinded.

Specification: Sick leave abuse in the First Quarter of 2024 (General Police Order 1.3.06).

Disposition: Found “Not Guilty” and the disciplinary action was dismissed.

Rationale: The member provided documentation for all sick leave taken while on Step-2 of the
Sick Leave Abuse Control Program.

Specification: Failed to report a Level 2 Use of Force (General Police Order 2.01.06).

Disposition: Found “Not Guilty” and the disciplinary action was dismissed.

Rationale: The member responded to a large fight where the scene was very chaotic and his
supervisor was injured. During the post incident investigation, it was determined that he did take
one of the combatants to the ground, a Level 2 Use of Force (within policy). The officer’s
recollection of the incident was that he and the combatant slipped and fell, and his Wearable
Camera System video was inconclusive as to what had occurred. Once a supervisor showed the
officer other video of the action taken, the officer did complete the required use of force report.

Specification: Sick leave abuse in the Second Quarter of 2024 (General Police Order 1.3.06).
Disposition: Found “Not Guilty” and the disciplinary action was dismissed.
Rationale: The member used 60 hours of sick leave and did provide documentation that covered
all but 24 hours of sick leave taken during the Second Quarter of 2024.

Specification: Workplace Violence policy (General Police Order 1.1.08).
Disposition: Found “Not Guilty” and the disciplinary action was dismissed.
Rationale: The Human Resource final report states: “While the behavior was not overtly
aggressive, the member’s actions, such as placing his vest on another officer’s desk without
reason, and his refusal to leave the office after being asked, appear to have been provocative”
and goes on to recommend review by the Division. I concur with the Human Resource report in
its determination that his actions leading to this incident do not comport with the standards and
expectations of employees in the Division of Police and Department of Public Safety. While the
member’s behavior, especially after being instructed by a superior to stop contact with the Patrol
Officer, was disruptive, it does not meet the specific definition for Workplace Violence.

Specification: Sick leave abuse in the First Quarter of 2025 (General Police Order 1.3.06).
Disposition: Found “Not Guilty” and the disciplinary action was dismissed.
Rationale: The member did provide documentation that covered all but 32 hours of sick leave
taken during the First Quarter of 2025.

Specification: Vehicle pursuit violation (General Police Order 3.2.02).
Disposition: Found “Not Guilty” and the disciplinary action was dismissed.
Rationale: The member was not the driver, and he was a Probationary Patrol Officer during this
incident.

Specification: Failed to supervise (Manual of Rules 1.06) – Lack of service (failed to notify 696-
KIDS) (Ohio Revised Code (ORC) 2151.421).

Disposition: Found “Not Guilty” and the disciplinary action was dismissed.

Rationale: The member conferred with a captain and received assistance from the NICE Unit
and a zone car, and established a plan to take the suspect into custody by having a patrol car use
its push-bar to keep the suspect vehicle from escaping down the driveway and had NICE Unit
officers provide a perimeter in the event there was a foot pursuit. He had several detectives and
the (uniformed) officers of Zone Car 3A37 on the passenger side of the vehicle as the de-
escalation technique – show of force. Upon finding the suspect asleep in the vehicle, the member
joined the other officers on the passenger side to eliminate cross-fire concerns. The suspect then
awoke, started the vehicle and attempted to go forward, but was unable to move forward due to
the member’s placement of the patrol car. The suspect then put the car in reverse and struck the
garage. Officers moved to positions of safety, with the exception of one officer who moved in
front of the suspect vehicle.

The member responded to a radio broadcast to assist officers regarding a child left unattended in
the back seat of a parked vehicle. Upon arriving at the scene, he met with patrol officers that had
already arrived. Neither the member nor the patrol officers saw the child unattended in the
vehicle. He did confer with the child’s mother, and learned that her father, the child’s
grandfather, had left the child unattended for about five minutes to go to the restroom, but before
she had returned from the restroom. This was in conflict with the witnesses, who stated the child
was left unattended for 20 minutes. The member then advised the mother and grandfather that
this was a serious matter and continued his investigation to ensure the safety of the child, and
found that the child was in good health. The member’s investigation, based upon nearly three
years of experience as a detective in the Cleveland Division of Police Sex Crimes and Child
Abuse Unit, found that he lacked the evidence required to pursue Ohio Revised Code §2919.22 –
Endangering Children or Cleveland Codified Ordinance §609.01 – Endangering Children. He
then ensured the child left the scene in a vehicle while properly secured in a child car seat.
Charges: Lack of Service,Asleep On-Duty,Failure to Supervise

Charge & Discipline Decision:
Specification: Failed to take proactive measures in dealing with an accidental THC ingestion
(Manual of Rules 4.14).

Disposition: Found “Not Guilty” and the disciplinary action was dismissed.

Rationale: The child’s mother was present and is ultimately responsible for her child’s well-
being. She was able to take any and all actions she felt appropriate for the care of her child. Any
deference she gave to the member’s opinion on the matter was of her own volition and no
evidence has been provided to indicate otherwise.

Specification: Sick leave abuse in the First Quarter of 2025 (General Police Order 1.3.06).

Disposition: Found “Not Guilty” and the disciplinary action was dismissed.

Rationale: The member provided documentation to her union, the Cleveland Police Patrolmen’s
Association that covered the 12 hours of sick leave taken on March 11, 2025.

Specification: Improper search (General Police Order 3.01.01) – Failure to supervise (Manual
of Rules 4.19).

Disposition: Found “Not Guilty” and the disciplinary action was dismissed.

Rationale: The member was assisting in the arrest of a combative male and gave a specific order
for the arrested male to be checked for a weapon. Wearable Camera System video showed that
he ordered the subordinate officer’s to, “Pat him down for a knife.”

Specification: Untruthful (Manual of Rules 3.12).
Disposition: Found “Not Guilty” and the disciplinary action was dismissed.
Rationale: The member stated that he was unable to contact the police after being involved in a
physical altercation with a citizen when phone records show that he made a phone call to an
involved witness prior to police arrival. The member had remotely completed a “factory reset”
on his cellphone and had not restored his phone until after the incident ended. Once his cellphone
had been restored, while still at the scene but in his vehicle, he had a 20 minute and 55 second
call and terminated the call as Cleveland Police officers arrived at the scene. Untruthfulness is
defined as the act of being intentionally deceitful, the willful perversion of the truth in order to
deceive, cheat, or defraud. Because this occurred after a highly stressful incident, insufficient
evidence was presented to indicate that the member was intentionally deceitful and his statement
was to deceive, cheat, or defraud, the evidence presented regarding his statement did not support
a violation of Untruthfulness.

The administrative charges were  dismissed. The Division accepted the resignation of Recruit Brooklyn Barnes.

Hearing Held By: Deputy Chief of Homeland Special Operations
Charge & Discipline Decision: Improper Procedure - Failed to place Wearable Camera  System (WCS) in event mode. The disciplinary action was Dismissed. However, Patrol Officer  Carl Dooley received a Non-Disciplinary, Corrective Letter of Re-Instruction.

Allowed a Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives (ATF)
special agent to bring a suspected live device to the Bomb Squad Unit for training purposes
(Bomb Squad Standard Operating Procedures, Response to Suspect or Suspicious Items, and
Manual of Rules 2.02) – Stored a suspected live device inside a truck that was parked inside the
Bomb Squad Unit’s garage prior to being transported to a safe location (Bomb Squad Standard
Operating Procedures, Duties and Responsibilities and Manual of Rules 2.02). Found “Not
Guilty” of storing a suspected live device at the Bomb Squad Unit’s garage. While the officer did
allow a suspected live device to be stored inside a truck that was parked inside the Bomb Squad
Unit’s garage prior to being transported to a safe location, there were no viable alternatives. The
Cleveland Division of Police’s Total Containment Vessel was not available, and there was no
access to the Ordnance Unit at the time of the incident. The officer did examine the live device
and determine it to be a grenade with missing components (i.e. switch, detonator, power source,
explosive load or charge) rendering the device inoperable. The officer also placed the grenade
into a “Frag Bag” which would have mitigated the blast and contained the fragments. This
specification was “Dismissed” and the Division will address the lack of alternatives that were
available to the officer. Because the officer immediately addressed the safety concerns by not
going forward with the training and had the ATF special agent remove the suspected live device,
this Group II violation was amended to a Group I violation. Only training devices are to be
utilized during training; in this instance a completely empty, sealed pipe could have been used.
Because the officer has no active discipline, the device was brought by the ATF for ATF led
training and the officer immediately addressed safety concerns, reinstruction is appropriate
regarding this training issue. The officer received a Non-disciplinary Letter of Reinstruction. The
letter is not discipline and should not be regarded as such.
Charges: Failure to Supervise,Failed to Safeguard Equipment

Charge & Discipline Decision:
Involved in a vehicle pursuit of a mini bike and failed to notify a superior
officer to respond to the scene to conduct the follow-up investigation (General Police Order
3.2.02) – Involved in a vehicle pursuit of a mini bike and entered their investigation of the
pursuit into BlueTeam (BlueTeam Quick Start Guide for Entering a Vehicle Pursuit and Manual conflict between General Police Order 3.2.02 and the BlueTeam Manual, reinstruction is
appropriate. The officer received a Non-disciplinary Letter of Reinstruction.
