Charge & Discipline Decision:
Specifications: While in an unmarked vehicle without activating the lights or siren, engaged in a
vehicle pursuit of a suspect who was not suspected of committing a violent felony and was not
suspected of Operating a Vehicle While Impaired, and drove at speeds reaching ninety-six miles
per hour on city streets. Failed to immediately notify the Communications Control Section (CCS)
that a pursuit was underway and failed terminate the pursuit when the suspect showed a blatant
disregard for the safety of other vehicles or pedestrians (General Police Order 3.2.02) –
Deactivated Wearable Camera System while engaged in a vehicle pursuit (General Police Order
4.06.04) – showed incompetence in decision making at the time of the incident while attempting
to explain the incident (Manual of Rules 2.14).

Disposition: The charge of incompetence in decision-making was dismissed. Found “Guilty” of
two Group I violations. Considering the officer’s active discipline history (none), the officer
received a three-day suspension. This corrective action is within the First Group I violation range
of the discipline matrix when aggravating factors (multiple violations arising from a single event
or incident may escalate the disciplinary action to the maximum group range) outweigh
mitigating factors (no active discipline, accepted responsibility).

Rationale: The officer pled “No Contest” and accepted responsibility. The inability of the
officer to articulate the reasoning behind his actions does not necessarily equate to incompetence
as judgment of those responses may be subjective. The officer intentionally deactivated his
Wearable Camera System (WCS) while engaged in a vehicle pursuit and engaged in a vehicle
pursuit of a suspect who was not suspected of committing a violent felony. The officer did not
immediately terminate the pursuit even though the suspect had a blatant disregard for the safety
of other vehicles or pedestrians.
Charges: WCS Violation,Vehicle Pursuit Violation,Failure to Report/Notify,Not Fit for Duty

Charge & Discipline Decision:
Specifications: Sick leave abuse in the Second Quarter of 2023 (General Police Order 1.3.06) –
Failed to safeguard and secure his assigned Divisional equipment (portable radio batteries)
(×2)(General Police Order 4.06.07 and Manual of Rules 6.05) – Failed to report the loss of City
property (Manual of Rules 6.06) – Untruthful (×6) (General Police Order 4.06.04 and Manual of
Rules 2.02, 2.07 and 3.12) – Failed to correct identified tagging errors to his Wearable Camera
System (WCS) downloads as ordered to by his Platoon Commander (×2) (General Police Order
4.06.04 and Manual of Rules 2.02 and 2.07) – Failed to familiarized himself with General Police
Order 4.06.04: Wearable Camera System (Manual of Rules 2.04) – Failed to report to duty as
assigned after being informed he was scheduled to work (Manual of Rules 2.02, 2.03 and 10.01)
– Refused to follow a direct order (Manual of Rules 2.02, 2.03 and 2.07) – Insubordination
(Manual of Rules 2.02, 2.03 and 2.06) – Failed to conduct missing person follow-ups and
complete supplement reports (General Police Order 6.2.17 and Manual of Rules 2.02, 2.03,
2.04, 4.18 and 9.12) – Failed to complete and submit reports prior to the end of his tour of duty
(General Police Order 6.2.17 and Manual of Rules 2.02, 2.03, 2.04, 9.08 and 9.12) – Failed to
properly tag and categorize Wearable Camera System videos (General Police Order 4.06.04 and
Manual of Rules 2.02, 2.03, and 2.04) – Failed to activate his Wearable Camera System prior to
responding to an incident (General Police Order 4.06.04) – Failed to correctly complete and
submit the OH-1 Crash Report and the Hit Skip Report (×3) (General Police Order 8.1.02,
8.1.04 and Manual of Rules 2.02, 2.03, 2.04, 9.08 and 9.12) – Failed to follow a lawful order
(Manual of Rules 2.02, 2.03, 2.04 and 2.06) – Sick leave abuse in the Third Quarter of 2023
(General Police Order 1.3.06) – Failed to provide medical documentation while on Step-1 of the
Sick Leave Abuse Control Program (General Police Order 1.3.06).

Disposition: Found “Not Guilty” of two charges of being untruthful and the insubordination
charge – the charges were dismissed. Found “Guilty” of fourteen Group I violations, two Group
II violations and seven Group III violations. Considering the officer’s active discipline history
(9/6/2023: five-day suspension/Step-1 sick abuse until 9/24/2024), the officer received a twenty-
five-day suspension. This corrective action is within the Group III Violation range of the
discipline matrix when aggravating factors (multiple violations, active discipline) outweigh
mitigating factors (accepted partial responsibility). In addition, the officer was placed on Step-2
of the Sick Leave Abuse Control Program until April 9, 2025, and is required to complete a
sixty-day “Work Performance” plan to ensure compliance in completing reports in a timely
manner and ensuring that all Wearable Camera System downloads have been property tagged.

Rationale: The officer accepted partial responsibility. On multiple occasions, the officer
exhibited a pattern of sick abuse, on two occasions he failed to safeguard his City-issued portable
radio batteries and failed to report the loss to his superior officer. The officer failed to correct
multiple tagging errors associated with his assigned Wearable Camera System (WCS) downloads
despite being given additional time and training to do so, and was Absent Without Leave
(AWOL) when he failed to report for duty as assigned. On multiple occasions, the officer failed
to complete and or correct reports in a timely manner and he failed to activate his Wearable
Camera System (WCS) prior to responding to an incident. He provided statements that were
determined to be untruthful when he completed a pre-structured Form-1 questionnaire regarding
WCS tagging errors and completing reports. Multiple violations arising from a single event or
incident may escalate the disciplinary action to the maximum group range.
Charges: Sick Leave Abuse,Absent Without Leave (AWOL),WCS Violation,Failed to Safeguard Equipment

Charge & Discipline Decision:
Specifications: Charged by the Cleveland Division of Animal Control in Cleveland Municipal
Court for violation of 603.01: Animals or Fowl at Large, 604.03B: Control of Dogs That Pose a
Safety Risk, and 604.04: Insurance; Signs; Notification. Charges 604.03B: Control of Dogs that
Pose a Safety Risk, and 604.04: Insurance; Signs; Notification, were Nolled. On July 13, 2022,
Cleveland Municipal Court found the member guilty of the minor misdemeanor offense 603.01:
Animals or Fowl at Large (Manual of Rules 2.01) – While off-duty, engaged in conduct that
would constitute the crime of Assault and was criminally charged for Ohio Revised Code
2903.11: Felonious Assault. (Manual of Rules 2.01).

Disposition: The charge of engaging in conduct that would constitute the crime of Assault more
closely comport with a Group II Violation (conduct that is contrary to the values of the Division,
or that interferes with its mission, operations or professional image, or that involves a
demonstrable serious risk to officer or public safety), the charge was amended from Group III
violation to a Group II violation. Found “Guilty” of one Group I violation and one Group II violation. 
Considering the member’s active discipline history (none) the member received a six-
day suspension. This corrective action is within the First Group II violation range of the
discipline matrix when mitigating factors (no active discipline, accepted partial responsibility)
outweigh aggravating factors (none).

Rationale: The member accepted partial responsibility. The member was found “Not Guilty” of
Ohio Revised Code 2093.11: Felonious Assault and the charge was dismissed. Her actions
contributed to the escalation of the incident and the preponderance of the evidence supports that
she did engage in acts that constitute Assault. As such, her behavior comports more closely to a
Group II violation of “Conduct Unbecoming” as she had multiple opportunities to de-escalate the
situation by disengaging and leaving the area rather than following the other party. In addition,
Cleveland Municipal Court found the member guilty of the minor misdemeanor offense 603.01:
Animals or Fowl at Large.
Charges: Conduct Unbecoming / Diminished Esteem,Arrest / Criminal Charge

addition, Patrol Officer Brandon Troutman is required to attend retraining in felony vehicle stops
by May 25, 2020.
Charges: WCS Violation,Failure to Report/Intervene (UOF),Failed to Request Medical Attention

Charge & Discipline Decision: Improper Procedure; De-escalation tactics. Patrol Officer  Kevin Berigan received a three (3) workday suspension without pay (Group I violation, second  Group I offense. Aggravating Factor: active discipline). In addition, Patrol Officer Kevin  Berigan is required to attend retraining in de-escalation by May 21, 2020.

Improper Procedure: involved in a motor vehicle accident  that was reviewed by the Traffic Safety Analysis Panel (TSAP) and determined to be preventable (x2). Mitigating Factor: none). In addition, he shall attend retraining in emergency response  driving and motor vehicle operation.

Aggravating Factors: multiple violations arising from a single incident escalate the disciplinary action to the maximum group range, violation of civil rights and supervisory rank of officer. Hearing Held By: Deputy Chief of Homeland Special Operations
Charge & Discipline Decision: Improper Procedure: improper handling of firearm (unsecured loaded firearm) - failed to carry two (2) intermediate weapons. Charge & Discipline Decision:
Improper Procedure: failed to assist fellow officer Wearable Camera System (WCS) violation (failed to place in event mode). Improper Procedure: failed to thoroughly search suspect – failed to conduct a thorough end of tour inspection. The Group II violation was amended to a Group I violation. Patrol Officer Michael Dunn received a two (2) workday suspension without pay (First Group I violation. Aggravating Factors: multiple violations arising from a single event or incident may escalate the disciplinary action to the maximum group range, failing to properly search a subject and failing to thoroughly inspect the zone car resulted in a firearm not being discovered - Mitigating Factors: accepted responsibility and no discipline history). In addition, Patrol Officer Michael Dunn is to attend retraining in search and seizure from the Training Section.

Specification: Discharged their service weapon during a use of deadly force incident and the
service rounds struck a multitude of occupied residential townhomes, creating a risk to
bystanders and residents (General Police Order 2.01.03).
Disposition: Found “Guilty” of one Group III violation. Considering the officer’s active
discipline history (none), the officer received a ten-day suspension. This corrective action is
within the First Group III violation range of the discipline matrix when mitigating factors
(rapidly evolving situation with an armed suspect, use of force was necessary, proportional and
objectively reasonable, no discipline history) outweigh aggravating factors (actions had the
potential to cause serious injury or harm to the public). In addition, the officer shall be retrained
in Firearms (surroundings, foreground, target, backstop & beyond) and Crime Scene
Management FIT procedures through the Training Section.
Rationale: The use of force was necessary, proportional and objectively reasonable during a
rapidly evolving situation with an armed suspect; the officer is responsible for every round
discharged from their firearm. The officer was trained to be aware of the surroundings, including
foreground, target, backstop and beyond. The fourteen rounds that were fired had the potential to
cause serious injury and harm to the public.
Charges: Use of Force Violation,Improper Handling of Firearm

Charge & Discipline Decision:
Specifications: Sick leave abuse in the first quarter of 2022 (General Police Order 1.3.06) –
Failed to complete a Form-1 as ordered and failed to report for duty after given the opportunity
to complete the Form-1 during the tour of duty (Manual of Rules 2.02, 2.06, 2.07, 10.01, and
General Police Order 1.3.06) – Failed to report for duty and was Absent Without Leave
(AWOL) from on or about June 1, 2022, through June 14, 2022 (Manual of Rules 2.02,2.06,
2.07, and 10.01) – Five hours and twenty-seven minutes late for duty (Manual of Rules 2.02,
2.03, 2.04, 2.14, 10.01, 10.02, Civil Service Commission Rule 9.10 Tenure and the City of
Cleveland Human Resources Policies and Procedures: Workplace Policies) – One hour and
fifty-one minutes late for duty (Manual of Rules 2.02, 2.03, 2.04, 2.14, 10.01, 10.02, Civil
Service Commission Rule 9.10 Tenure and the City of Cleveland Human Resources Policies and
Procedures: Workplace Policies) – Eleven minutes late for duty (Manual of Rules 2.02, 2.03,
2.04, 2.14, 10.01, 10.02, Civil Service Commission Rule 9.10 Tenure and the City of Cleveland
Human Resources Policies and Procedures: Workplace Policies). 

Disposition: Found “Guilty” of three Group I violations, one Group II violation, and two Group
III violations. Considering the officer’s active discipline history (1/10/2022 – one-day
suspension, 4/19/2022 – three-day suspension), the officer received a seventeen-day suspension
and was placed on Step-1 of the Sick Leave Abuse Control Program until November 10, 2023.
This corrective action is within the First Group III violation range of the discipline matrix when
aggravating factors (active discipline, multiple violations arising from a single event or incident
may escalate the disciplinary action to the maximum group range) outweigh mitigating factors
(accepted responsibility).
Rationale: The Bilingual Communications Specialist used ninety-six hours of undocumented
sick time and displayed a pattern of sick use that preceded or followed scheduled vacation days
or compensatory time during the First Quarter of 2022, was insubordinate by failing to complete
a Form-1 as ordered by a superior officer, was absent without leave (AWOL) on June 1, 2022,
June 4, 2022, June 6, 2022, June 9, 2022, and June 14, 2022, and was late for duty on three
separate occasions.
Charges: Sick Leave Abuse,Failure to Report/Notify,Absent Without Leave (AWOL),Tardiness

Hearing Held By: Deputy Chief of Homeland Special Operations
Charge & Discipline Decision:
Specification: Failed to avoid taking unnecessary actions that may escalate the need to use force
when the detective used a harsh level of voice and tone. (General Police Order 2.01.02 and
Manual of Rules 5.01, 5.08 and 5.09).
Disposition: Found “Guilty” of one Group I violation. Considering the officer’s active discipline history (2/17/2020 – one-day suspension), the officer received a two-day suspension. This
discipline is within the First Group I violation range of the discipline matrix when aggravating
factors (active discipline) outweigh mitigating factors (uncooperative suspect). In addition, the
officer shall be retrained in de-escalation techniques through the Training Section.

Rationale: The officer did not use strategic communication or voice commands to de-escalate
the situation and did not calmly verbalize to the complainant in a normal tone of voice when they
threatened to break the complainant’s “(expletive) window” and threatened to “tase” him.
Charges: Use of Force Violation,Failed to De-escalate

Hearing Held By: "Assistant Director of Public Safety, Administrative Operations"
Charge & Discipline Decision:
Specification: Discharged service weapon during a use of deadly force incident. After the
application of force in discharging their weapon, the officer’s service rounds struck a multitude
of occupied residential townhomes, creating a risk to bystanders and residents (General Police
Order 2.01.03).
Disposition: Found “Guilty” of one Group III violation. Considering the officer’s active
discipline history (8/9/2021: six-day suspension, 11/3/2021: eight-day suspension and Written
Reprimand, 9/16/2022: ten-day suspension), the officer received a twenty-day suspension. This
corrective action is within the First Group III violation range of the discipline matrix when aggravating factors (active discipline, actions that had the potential to cause serious injury or
harm to the public) outweigh mitigating factors (the rapidly evolving situation with an armed
suspect, use of force was necessary, proportional and objectively reasonable). In addition, the
officer shall be retrained in Firearms (surroundings, foreground, target, backstop & beyond) and
Crime Scene Management FIT procedures through the Training Section.
Rationale: The use of force was necessary, proportional and objectively reasonable during a
rapidly evolving situation with an armed suspect; the officer is responsible for every round that
they discharge from their firearm. The officer was trained to be aware of their surroundings,
including foreground, target, backstop and beyond. The eight (8) rounds that were fired had the
potential to cause serious injury and harm to the public.
Charges: Use of Force Violation,Improper Handling of Firearm

Charge & Discipline Decision:
Specifications: Derelict in their duties when the officer failed to assist their partner, who was in
a physical struggle with a male that was actively resisting (Manual of Rules 1.07 and 4.11) – Failed to notify Communications Control Section (CCS) when their partner was in a physical
struggle with a male that was actively resisting (Manual of Rules 1.07 and 4.10) – Violated the
Cleveland Division of Police grooming standards when the officer was observed with hair
extending below the lower edge of the shirt collar (General Police Order 1.04.01 and Manual of
Rules 1.07, 2.02, 2.03 and 2.04).
Disposition: Found “Guilty” of two Group I violations and one Group II violation. Considering
the officer’s active discipline history (2/15/2021: one-day suspension), the officer received an
eight-day suspension. This discipline is within the First Group II violation range of the discipline
matrix when aggravating factors (multiple violations, active discipline) outweigh mitigating
factors (none). In addition, the officer shall be retrained in subject control through the Training
Section.
Rationale: The officer failed to notify the Communications Control Section and assist their
partner, who was in a physical struggle with an actively resisting male suspect. This failure
continued as the suspect was attempting to get their partner’s Conducted Electrical Weapon
(CEW). The WCS video also indicated that the officer’s hair extended below the lower edge of
their uniform shirt collar.
Charges: Failed to Assist,Failure to Report/Notify,Uniform/Grooming Violation

Hearing Held By: Deputy Chief of Homeland Special Operations
Charge & Discipline Decision:
Failed to obtain basic response information before transferring a caller to EMS  (Manual of Rules 2.02 and CCS Policy and Procedures 1.1.17) – Answered a non-emergency  call with suspects on scene at an after-hours location; during the call the member was heard  snoring on a recorded line throughout the call and also did not create an assignment to have  police respond to the complaint (Manual of Rules 4.03 and 5.12). 

Found “Guilty” of one Group I violation and one Group II violation. Considering  the officer’s active discipline history (none), the member received a six-day suspension for  sleeping on-duty. In addition, the member received a Non-disciplinary Letter of Reinstruction for  failing to obtain basic response information. This corrective action is within the First Group II  violation range of the discipline matrix when mitigating factors (no active discipline, accepted  responsibility) outweigh aggravating factors (none).

The officer pled “No Contest” and accepted responsibility. The member was sleeping  while on-duty, on a recorded line. After a Quality Assurance Review of an incident where the  member received a 911 call for “shots fired,” she failed to obtain basic response information  before transferring the caller to EMS.
Charges: Lack of Service,Conduct Unbecoming / Diminished Esteem

Charge & Discipline Decision:
Specification: Not courteous in speech, conduct and contact with citizens (Manual of Rules 5.01,
5.08 and 5.09) – Violated the uniform and clothing regulation of the Division of Police (General
Police Order 1.1.12).

Disposition: The officer pled “No Contest” and accepted responsibility. Considering the
officer’s active discipline history (none), he received a six-day suspension for the discourteous
speech and conduct. In addition, the officer received a Non-disciplinary Letter of Reinstruction
for the remaining charge. This corrective action is within the First Group II violation range of the
discipline matrix when mitigating factors (accepted responsibility, no active discipline) outweigh
aggravating factors (multiple violations). In addition, it is recommended the officer follow-up
with the Employee Assistance Unit.

Rationale: The officer was discourteous and did reasonably diminish the esteem of the Division
of Police in the eyes of the public with the language used, both while en route to the incident,
and then while on the scene. The officer did not meet the dress code standards when he wore his
Cleveland Police baseball cap backwards on his head while riding in the patrol car while en route
to an assignment.

Hearing Held By: "Assistant Director of Public Safety, Administrative Operations"
Charge & Discipline Decision:
Specification: Failed to report a Use of Force to an on-duty supervisor in a timely manner while
working secondary employment (General Police Order 2.01.05) – Failed to deescalate (General
Police Order 2.01.02) – Failed to have approval to engage in Secondary Employment (General
Police Order 1.02.13).

Disposition: Found “Guilty” of two Group I violations and one Group II violation. Considering
the officer’s active discipline history (8/9/2021: 6-day suspension, 11/3/2021: 8-day suspension,
9/16/2022: 10-day suspension, 11/4/2022: 20-day suspension), he received a thirteen-day
suspension. Because this is the officer’s third sustained Group II violation within a three-year
period, the discipline automatically progresses into the next higher group (Group III violation
range). This corrective action is within the first Group III violation range of the discipline matrix
when aggravating factors (multiple violations, active discipline) outweigh mitigating factors
(none). In addition, the officer received a Non-disciplinary Letter of Reinstruction for the failing
to deescalate charge.

Rationale: The officer should have known force was used in the manner the officer took the
subject to the ground, and therefore he should have reported it as soon as practical. The officer
did work unauthorized secondary employment. In addition, the officer also did not employ
proper de-escalation techniques when he pointed his finger in the subject’s face and failed to
maintain reactionary space, reinstruction is appropriate regarding this instance.
Charges: Failure to Report/Intervene (UOF),Failed to De-escalate,Unauthorized Secondary Employment

Charge & Discipline Decision:
Specification: Called off sick and failed to provide documentation to the Medical Director while
on Step-1 of the Sick Abuse Program (×5) (General Police Order 1.3.06) – Failed to forward a
felony report prior to completing her tour of duty (Manual of Rules 2.02, 2.03, 9.04, 9.05, 9.08
and 9.12) – Insubordinate to a superior officer (Manual of Rules 2.06) – Using sick time under
circumstances that clearly indicate it was being used to obtain time off work that is unrelated to
sickness, injury, a doctor/dental visit, serious health condition of family member, or pregnancy
(General Police Order 1.3.06).

Disposition: Found “Guilty” of seven Group I violations and one Group II violation.
Considering the officer’s active discipline history (11/1/2023: Step-1 sick abuse, 1/20/2023: 10-
day suspension, 4/5/2023: Step-2 sick abuse), the officer received a fourteen-day suspension.
This corrective action is within the second Group I and first Group II violation range of the
discipline matrix when aggravating factors (multiple violations, active discipline) outweigh
mitigating factors (none). In addition, the officer received a Non-disciplinary Letter of
Reinstruction placing her on Step-3 of the Sick Leave Abuse Control Program until August 23,
2024.

Rationale: In the confrontation regarding failing to complete a report, the officer used
unacceptable language towards a superior officer; however, because this behavior more closely
comports with a Group II Violation (conduct that is contrary to the values of the Division, or that
interferes with its mission, operations or professional image, or that involves a demonstrable
serious risk to officer or public safety), the charge was amended from a Group III violation to a
Group II violation. The officer violated the provisions of the Step-1 Sick Abuse Program when
she failed to provide documentation upon her return from sick leave on multiple occasions and
failed to forward a felony report prior to completing her tour of duty. After being confronted
about failing to complete a report the previous day, she completed the report and then used sick
leave outside of policy.

Hearing Held By: Deputy Chief of Homeland Special Operations
Charge & Discipline Decision:
Specifications: Sick leave abuse in the Second Quarter of 2023 (General Police Order 1.3.06) –
Failed to appear as ordered to a pre-disciplinary hearing (Manual of Rules 2.02 and 10.01) – Sick
leave abuse in the Third Quarter of 2023 (General Police Order 1.3.06).

Disposition: The charge of failing to appear as ordered to a pre-disciplinary hearing more
closely comports with a Group I Violation (conduct that has a negative impact on the operations
or professional image of the Division or that negatively impacts relationships with other officers,
agencies or the public), the charge was amended from a Group II violation to a Group I violation.
Found “Guilty” of three Group I violations. Considering the member’s active discipline history
(none) the member received a two-day suspension. This discipline is within the First Group I
violation range of the discipline matrix when aggravating factors (multiple violations) outweigh
mitigating factors (no active discipline, accepted responsibility). In addition, the member
received a Non-disciplinary Letter of Reinstruction placing her on Step-1 of the Sick Leave
Abuse Control Program until January 4, 2025.

Rationale: The member accepted responsibility. During the Second Quarter of 2023; the
member used one-hundred-twenty-five hours of undocumented sick time and during the Third
Quarter of 2023; used fifty-three hours of undocumented sick time. The member did not read the
letter in its entirety and failed to attend her pre-disciplinary hearing because she was not aware of
the hearing. Multiple violations arising from a single event or incident may escalate the
disciplinary action to the maximum group range
Charges: Sick Leave Abuse,Attendance and Overtime Violations

Charge & Discipline Decision:
Specifications: Called in sick five minutes before his scheduled start time (Manual of Rules
5.01) – Failed to submit a Daily Duty Report (×6) (General Police Order 4.04.03) – Failed to
obey a direct order (Manual of Rules 2.02) – Failed to sign out patrol car as required (Manual of
Rules 2.02) – Used a patrol car and left it completely empty of fuel (Divisional Notice 20-117
and Manual of Rules 1.07 and 2.02) – Absent without leave (AWOL) after being denied
compensatory time off (General Police Order 4.04.01 and Manual of Rules 2.02, 10.01 and
10.02).

Disposition: Found “Guilty” of three Group I violations, one Group II violation, and two Group
III violations. The officer received a Non-disciplinary Letter of Reinstruction for failing to sign
out a patrol car and using a patrol car and left it devoid of fuel. Considering the officer’s active
discipline history (none) the officer received a fifteen-day suspension for the remaining charges.
This corrective action is within the Group III violation range of the discipline matrix when
mitigating factors (accepted total responsibility, no active discipline) outweigh aggravating
factors (multiple violations).

Rationale: The officer pled “No Contest” and accepted responsibility. The officer failed to sign
a patrol car out as required and left it completely empty of fuel, reinstruction is appropriate for
these instances. The officer called in sick five minutes prior to his scheduled start time and
caused the Division to not be able to conduct pre-scheduled ALICE training, failed to submit a
Daily Duty Report at the completion of at least six tours of duty, did not obey a direct order from
his Acting Commander to submit a Daily Duty Report each day, and was absent without leave by
not reporting for duty as assigned after being denied compensatory time off. Multiple violations
arising from a single event or incident may escalate the disciplinary action to the maximum
group range.
Charges: Sick Leave Abuse,Absent Without Leave (AWOL),Duty Report Violation,Travel Violation,Insubordination

Charge & Discipline Decision:
Specifications: Used force that was not proportional (General Police Order 2.01.03) –
Allegation of Excessive Force (General Police Order 2.01.03) – Allegation of Improper
Procedure/Duty to Avoid the Appearance of Impropriety (General Police Order 1.3.33).

Disposition: The officer received a Non-disciplinary Letter of Reinstruction for the Improper
Procedure/Duty to Avoid the Appearance of Impropriety charge; the remaining charges were
merged, as they were similar in nature. Found “Guilty” of one Group III violation. Considering
the officer’s active discipline history (none), the officer received a thirteen-day suspension for
the remaining charge. This corrective action is within the First Group III violation range of the
discipline matrix when aggravating factors (supervisory rank of officer) outweigh mitigating
factors (no active discipline).

Rationale: The officer used force that was not proportional during the course of an arrest
resulting in the serious physical injury. Regarding the Duty to Avoid the Appearance of
Impropriety, the officer was given permission by two higher ranking supervisors to participate in
an investigation but failed to provide the request in writing, reinstruction is appropriate in this
instance.
Charges: Use of Force Violation,Appearance of Impropriety

Charge & Discipline Decision:
Specifications: Sick leave abuse in the Third Quarter of 2023 (General Police Order 1.3.06) –
Failed to terminate a pursuit when ordered to do so by a supervisor (General Police Order
3.2.02).

Disposition: Found “Guilty” of one Group I violation and one Group II violation. The officer
received a Non-disciplinary Letter of Reinstruction for sick leave usage in the Third Quarter of
2023 and he will continue on Step-1 of the Sick Leave Abuse Control Program until December
13, 2024, as stated in his disposition letter dated December 13, 2023. Considering the officer’s
active discipline history (none), the officer received a six-day suspension for the remaining
charge. This corrective action is within the first Group II violation range of the discipline matrix
when mitigating factors (no active discipline, accepted responsibility) outweigh aggravating
factors (none).

Rationale: The officer pled “No Contest” and accepted responsibility. The officer was instructed
to terminate a pursuit and did not terminate as ordered. The officer’s use of sick time during the
Third Quarter of 2023 and the two-month look back period requires further monitoring,
reinstruction is appropriate in this instance.

Specifications: Engaged in secondary employment while on sick leave (General Police Orders
1.02.13 and 1.3.06) – Sick leave abuse in the Fourth Quarter of 2023 (General Police Order
1.3.06).

Disposition: Found “Guilty” of one Group I violation and one Group II violation. Considering
the officer’s active discipline history (none), the officer received a six-day suspension. This
corrective action is within the first Group II violation range of the discipline matrix when
mitigating factors (no active discipline, accepted responsibility) outweigh aggravating factors
(none). In addition, the officer received a Non-disciplinary Letter of Reinstruction placing him
on Step-1 of the Sick Leave Abuse Control Program until April 19, 2025.

Rationale: The officer did work secondary employment concurrent with being on sick leave
with the Cleveland Division of Police and did exhibit a pattern of sick time abuse, to wit: during
the Fourth Quarter of 2023, he used forty hours of undocumented sick leave, with all forty hours
attached to V-Days. Additionally, during the two-month look back period, he used twenty hours
of undocumented sick leave, with ten hours attached to a V-Day and the other ten hours on a
weekend.

Specifications: Closed out an un-dispatched incident without a supervisors approval
(Communications Control Section Policy and Procedures, General Dispatching Procedures
1.1.18) – Closed out an un-dispatched incident when the business was open for another two
hours (Communications Control Section Policy and Procedures, General Dispatching
Procedures, Floor Operations 1.1.18, Section X. C. 1, 2, 3, and 4) – Did not advise a supervisor
prior to closing out an incident (Communications Control Section Policy and Procedures,
General Dispatching Procedures, Floor Operations 1.1.18, Section IV, Downgrading and
Closing Incidents and Policy and Procedures, Call Taking Procedures, Floor Operations 1.1.17,
Section IX, A, 1, 2) – In a “not ready” status for twenty-six minutes (Communications Control
Section Policy and Procedures, General Policies and Prohibitions, Personnel, 1.3.01 Section 16)
– Asleep on-duty (Manual of Rules 5.12) – Advised of an overtime mandate and left on sick
leave (General Police Order 1.3.06) – Refused mandatory overtime on five occasions (Manual
of Rules 10.01) – Called off sick after being denied the use of compensatory time (General
Police Order 1.3.06).

Disposition: The charge of closing out an un-dispatched incident and failing to advise a
supervisor prior to closing out an incident were merged. Found “Guilty” of six Group I violations
and one Group II violation. Considering the member’s active discipline history (none), the
member received a six-day suspension. This corrective action is within the first Group II
violation range of the discipline matrix when aggravating factors (multiple violations) outweigh
mitigating factors (no active discipline). In addition, the member received a Non-disciplinary
Letter of Reinstruction placing her on Step-1 of the Sick Leave Abuse Control Program until
April 29, 2025.

Rationale: The member accepted partial responsibility. The member did fail to assign an
incident while it was the only assignment pending, was in a “not ready” status for twenty-six
minutes and was observed to be asleep on-duty while at her assigned telephone position. The
member was advised that she was to be mandated; she then stated that she was going home
“sick.” She used sick leave after being denied compensatory time off and refused mandatory
overtime on four occasions (October 20, 2023 and December 14, 23 and 24, 2023) – this exceeds
the single mandatory refusal per quarter, as contractually allowed. Multiple violations arising
from a single event or incident may escalate the disciplinary action to the maximum group range.
Charges: Sick Leave Abuse,Refusal of Mandatory Overtime,Asleep On-Duty,Lack of Service,Neglect of Duty

Charge & Discipline Decision:
Specifications: Unprofessional behavior (tone of voice) (unprofessional tone) (Communications
Control Section Policy and Procedures, Call Taking Procedures 1.1.17) – Failed to initiate an
incident (Communications Control Section Policy and Procedures, Call Taking Procedures
1.1.17 and Manual of Rules 4.03) – Failed to provide medical documentation to the Medical
Director while on Step-2 of the Sick Leave Abuse Control Program (General Police Order
1.3.06).

Disposition: Found “Guilty” of four Group I violations. Considering the member’s active
discipline history (none), the member received a five-day suspension. This corrective action is
within the first Group I violation range of the discipline matrix when aggravating factors
(multiple violations may escalate the disciplinary action to the maximum group range) outweigh
mitigating factors (no active discipline). In addition, the member’s placement on Step-2 of the
Sick Leave Abuse Control Program is extended until April 25, 2025.

Rationale: The member accepted partial responsibility. While working a Call Station, the
member received a Non-Emergency Call and displayed unprofessional behavior, to wit: her tone
of voice indicated that she was bothered by the caller not answering her question; she terminated
the call improperly when she advised the caller that she was disconnecting the call while the
caller was still talking without asking clarifying or safety questions (such as number of suspects
or suspect descriptions). The member disconnected a call, failed to initiate an incident,
unnecessarily raising her voice and failed to note pertinent information. Multiple violations
arising from a single event or incident may escalate the disciplinary action to the maximum
group range.
Charges: Sick Leave Abuse,Unprofessional Conduct,Lack of Service

Charge & Discipline Decision:
Specifications: Level 3 force (not necessary, not proportional) (General Police Order 2.01.03) –
Discharged service weapon (General Police Order 2.01.03).
Disposition: The charge of discharging her service weapon was merged into the Level 3 force
charge. Found “Guilty” of one Group III violation. Considering the member’s active discipline
history (none), the member received a ten-day suspension. This corrective action is within the
first Group III Violation range of the discipline matrix when mitigating factors (no active
discipline, believed gunfire was headed in her direction, very rapidly evolving situation
requiring split-second decision making) outweigh aggravating factors (actual or demonstrable
legal or financial risk to the Division or the City). In addition, the member will receive training
regarding the prohibited use of deadly force towards a motor vehicle when a direct threat, outside
of a vehicle, is not identified.
Rationale: The charge of discharging her service weapon is a result of the action noted in the
Level 3 force charge; they were merged. The member and her partner were gathering
information from a radio assignment of threats – suspect on scene, and during her interview of
the complainant, the complainant indicated that she believed that damage to her daughter’s
vehicle came from a bullet striking the vehicle’s windshield. The complainant proceeded to show
the member broken glass from the reportedly shot-out car window in the driveway of her
residence, and advised that she did not know the whereabouts of her daughter, who was not at
the residence. The complainant further expressed concern over the potential for harm to anyone
in her home by stating that her brother was sleeping on the couch and could have been shot if the
person she suspected had shot through the house. Just prior to clearing this call for service, a
large volley of gunshots was heard coming from the area approximately 500 feet from the
member’s location. Moments later, as a vehicle (Ford Fusion) was headed toward her location, a
second volley of gunshots was fired. The member reported that she believed the gunfire was
coming from the Ford Fusion and that she was now in fear for her life. Video captured the
member moving to the front driver’s side of her patrol car and discharging her City-issued pistol
four times toward the perceived threat. The Ford Fusion struck the passenger side of the patrol
car and continued down the street, away from her location. The member had discharged her
firearm at a threat that was not verified and visible as is required. Multiple violations arising
from a single event or incident may escalate the disciplinary action to the maximum group range.
Charges: Use of Force Violation,Improper Handling of Firearm

Charge & Discipline Decision:
Specification: Abusive/demeaning language (Manual of Rules 5.09).

Disposition: The charge more closely comports with a Group I Violation (conduct that has a
negative impact on the operations or professional image of the Division or that negatively
impacts relationships with other officers, agencies or the public); the charge was amended from a
Group II violation to a Group I violation. Found “Guilty” of one Group I violation. The original
discipline (one-day suspension) was grieved, and Labor Relations issued a decision; the member
received a Non-disciplinary Letter of Reinstruction.

Rationale: The member had engaged in a level 2 use of force incident with a juvenile suspect
who had what appeared to be a pistol partially concealed in his pants. Upon gaining control of
the juvenile suspect and retrieving what he had determined to be a facsimile firearm, the member
stated to the juvenile suspect, “God damn, you f***ing stupid.” This speech is outside division
expectations and more closely comports with a Group I violation (discourtesy/rudeness) than the
Group II violation (abusive/demeaning language).

Specifications: Called an arrested male a racial slur (Manual of Rules 5.09 and 5.11) – Unsafe
handling of a shotgun (Manual of Rules 5.13).

Disposition: The charge of calling an arrested male a racial slur more closely comport with a
Group I violation (conduct that has a negative impact on the operations or professional image of
the Division or that negatively impacts relationships with other officers, agencies or the public);
the charge was amended from a Group III violation to a Group I violation. Found “Guilty” of one
Group I violation and one Group II violation. Considering the member’s active discipline history
(none) the member received a six-day suspension for the unsafe handling of the patrol car’s
assigned pump action 12-guage shotgun. In addition, the member received a Non-disciplinary
Letter of Reinstruction for the remaining charge. This corrective action is within the first Group
II Violation range of the discipline matrix when mitigating factors (no active discipline)
outweigh aggravating factors (the existence of an injury risk to officers or bystanders).

Rationale: The member removed the shotgun from the vehicle’s shotgun mount and left the
shotgun unattended/unsecured in the front seat of the vehicle – an area that the arrested male had
access to and soon moved to, escalating the danger to anyone in the vicinity. The member did
make unprofessional comments; however, the subject of the comments was not African
American, the statement did not meet the requirements of Manual of Rules 5.11, “epithets, terms
or words which tend to denigrate any person(s) due to their race, color, creed, ethnicity, country
or region of origin, or sexual orientation...”
Charges: Offensive Remarks,Conduct Unbecoming / Diminished Esteem,Improper Handling of Firearm

Charge & Discipline Decision:
Specifications: Wearable Camera System (WCS) violation (×3) (General Police Order 4.06.04
and Divisional Notice 25-158) – Failed to notify radio dispatch (×2) (Manual of Rules 7.04) –
Used an electronic wireless communications device while operating a motor vehicle (×2) (Ohio
Revised Code 4511.204, Cleveland Codified Ordinance 431.34 and Manual of Rules 2.01) –
Operated a Cleveland Police vehicle that was involved in a motor vehicle crash that was
reviewed by the Traffic Safety Analysis Panel (TSAP) and determined to be preventable
(Manual of Rules 4.03, 6.03 and 6.05) – Initiated a vehicle pursuit that did not meet the approved
criteria and failed to advise the Communications Control Section (CCS) or a supervisor (General
Police Order 3.2.02) – Failed to clear assignment (General Police Order 7.01.02) – Emergency
response driving violation (General Police Order 4.01.01) – Failed to complete an entry in the
log each time a patrol rifle was placed in or removed from storage (General Police Order
4.06.08) – Stored a City Patrol Rifle in a personal locker (General Police Order 4.06.08).
Disposition: Found “Guilty” of thirteen Group I violations. Considering the member’s active
discipline history (none) the member received a Letter of Reinstruction for the preventable motor
vehicle crash and a five-day suspension for the remaining charges. This corrective action is
within the Group I violation range of the discipline matrix when aggravating factors (multiple
violations arising from a single event or incident may escalate the disciplinary action to the
maximum group range, created safety risk to the public and Divisional members) outweigh
mitigating factors (no active discipline, accepted partial responsibility).
Rationale: The member operated a patrol car while on-duty that was involved in a motor vehicle
crash in which the Traffic Safety Analysis Panel (TSAP) determined to be preventable. The
member failed to activate his Wearable Camera System (WCS) and in-car camera prior to taking
investigative or enforcement actions and did fail to notify the Communications Control Section
(CCS) while investigating a stolen vehicle on July 28, 2025. The member used a wireless
communications device (cell phone) while operating a motor vehicle to contact other Divisional
members on July 28, 2025, and July 30, 2025, while the City radio system was functioning
appropriately. The member initiated a vehicle pursuit that did not meet the approved criteria and
failed to advise the Communications Control Section (CCS) or a supervisor. The member failed
to activate his Wearable Camera System (WCS) prior to taking investigative or enforcement
action with the public and failed to advise a radio dispatcher of his activities on July 30, 2025.
The member failed to clear a parking violator assignment that he was currently assigned while
being involved in a vehicle pursuit and engaged in emergency response driving without
emergency lights and audible siren activated on July 30, 2025. His operation while engaged in
emergency response driving included driving left of center, driving on the wrong side of the
street, over-taking traffic, and high rates of speed, creating a risk to the safety of the public and
other Divisional members. The member failed to activate his Wearable Camera System (WCS)
prior to responding to a call for service on July 31, 2025. In addition, the member failed to
complete an entry in the log each time a City patrol rifle was placed in or removed from storage
and did store a City patrol rifle in his personal locker.
Charges: WCS Violation,Failure to Report/Notify,Telecommunications Violation,Preventable Motor Vehicle Accident (MVA),Vehicle Pursuit Violation,Travel Violation,Improper Handling of Firearm

Charge & Discipline Decision:
Specifications: Used unnecessary force on a citizen, by placing his knee on the citizen's head,
and again used unnecessary force when he pushed the citizen’s head to the ground, causing
injury (General Police Order 2.01.03 and Manual of Rules 4.05) – Used force on a citizen and
did not identify and report the level 3 use of force to a supervisor (General Police Order 2.01.05)
– Failed to place his Wearable Camera System (WCS) into event mode before engaging in a foot
pursuit (General Police Order 4.06.04) – Failed to place his Wearable Camera System (WCS)
into an Evidence Transfer Manager (ETM) slot by the end of his tour of duty causing a total of at
least 92 WCS recordings to be delayed in uploading for up to four months, and at least 32 WCS
recordings to be delayed in uploading for up to 26 days (General Police Order 4.06.04) –
Conducted a strip search on a male where he failed to explain to him the reason for the search,
did not get written pre-approval from a police supervisor, and did not complete a Cleveland
Division of Police Prisoner Search Report (General Police Order 2.02.04) – Was aware that a
male was having difficulty breathing after a use of de-minimus force and failed to obtain medical
assistance (General Police Order 2.01.03 and Manual of Rules 4.14) – Was one of the primary
detectives in a stop, detention, and arrest of a male and failed to complete a Stop Form (General
Police Order 2.02.05).
Disposition: The charges of using unnecessary force on a citizen and conducting a strip search
were dismissed. Found “Guilty” of five Group I violations. Considering the officer’s active
discipline history (12/27/2021: 2-day suspension), the officer received a three-day suspension for
failing to place his Wearable Camera System into event mode before engaging in a foot pursuit
and received a Non-disciplinary Letter of Reinstruction for the remaining charges. This
corrective action is within the Second Group I violation range of the discipline matrix when
aggravating factors (active discipline) outweigh mitigating factors (none). In addition, the officer
shall attend retraining through the Training Section on Use of Force Reporting and Procedures.
Rationale: The contact between the officer’s knee and the head of the suspect was momentary
and incidental as a result of the suspect being taken into custody, and a review of the incident
indicates that a "strip search" did not occur. The officer did fail to place his Wearable Camera
System into event mode before engaging in a foot pursuit. In addition, he failed on multiple
occasions to place his WCS into an ETM slot by the end of his tour of duty; reinstruction is
appropriate regarding this instance. The officer reported the use of force incident to his
supervisor on-scene and described what he believed to be his use of force – his lack of
knowledge concerning uses of force caused him to incorrectly report the level of force;
reinstruction is appropriate regarding this instance. The officer failed to obtain medical
assistance; however, the suspect did not suffer any visible or incapacitating injuries as a result of
his actions and a Stop Form was not completed; reinstruction is appropriate regarding these
instances.
Charges: WCS Violation,Improper Search/Frisk,Use of Force Violation,Failure to Report/Intervene (UOF),Failed to Request Medical Attention

Charge & Discipline Decision:
Specifications: Improperly stopped a citizen openly carrying a firearm (General Police Order
2.02.01, Manual of Rules 1.06, 2.02, 2.03, 2.04 and 5.01, and Divisional Notice 21-026) –
Directed a citizen to be improperly arrested (Ohio Revised Code 2923.12 and 2917.31, Manual
of Rules 1.06, 2.02, 2.03, 2.04 and 5.01, and Divisional Notice 21-026) – Failed to complete a
Use of Force Report and a Stop Form (General Police Orders 2.01.05 and 2.02.05) – Improperly
towed a citizen’s vehicle (General Police Order 6.03.01).

Disposition: The officer was found “Not Guilty” of the charges of improperly stopping a citizen
and improperly towing a citizen’s vehicle, as such, the charges were dismissed. Found “Guilty”
of one Group I violation and one Group III violation. The officer received a Non-disciplinary
Letter of Reinstruction for failing to complete a Use of Force Report and a Stop Form.
Considering the officer’s active discipline history (none), the officer received a thirteen-day
suspension for the remaining charge. This corrective action is within the Group III violation
range of the discipline matrix when aggravating factors (supervisory rank) outweigh mitigating
factors (no active discipline).

Rationale: There were multiple shooting incidents that occurred and in close proximity to the
location that the “stop” occurred and it was the officer’s responsibility to investigate these
incidents with due diligence. Since the stop was not improper and the subject was arrested, the
subsequent tow of the vehicle was not improper. The officer did fail to complete a Use of Force
Report and a Stop Form; reinstruction is appropriate regarding this instance. It was incumbent
upon the officer to investigate the multiple shootings that occurred during the time and in close
proximity of his location, once it was determined that no crime had taken place, the officer’s
engagement with the citizen should have ended.
Charges: Improper Arrest/Detainment,Failure to Report/Intervene (UOF),Improper Stop,Improper Tow

observed on Real Time Crime Center video following behind the primary and secondary pursuit

Disposition: Found “Guilty” of two Group I violations. Considering the officer’s active

danger of pursuing in an unmarked vehicle) outweigh mitigating factors (no active discipline). In
addition, the officer received a Written Reprimand for the Wearable Camera System violation.
This corrective action is within the first Group I violation range of the discipline matrix when

Rationale: A review of the Real Time Crime Center video revealed that the officer was directly
involved with a vehicle pursuit without supervisory approval and did not activate his Wearable
Camera System upon receiving the assignment to assist (in an auxiliary role) regarding the
pursuit.

Specifications: Involved in a vehicle pursuit without supervisory approval when he was

vehicles (General Police Order 3.2.02) – Failed to place his Wearable Camera System (WCS)
into event mode while actively involved in a vehicle pursuit (General Police Order 4.06.04).

Disposition: Found “Guilty” of two Group I violations. Considering the officer’s active

violation range of the discipline matrix when aggravating factors (unnecessary and inherent
danger of pursuing in an unmarked vehicle) outweigh mitigating factors (no active discipline). In
addition, the officer received a Written Reprimand for the Wearable Camera System violation.

Specifications: Negligently caused a revolver to discharge in the presence of citizens and a
fellow Cleveland Division of Police member (General Police Order 5.08.01 and Manual of
Rules 4.03) – Removed his Wearable Camera System (WCS) from his outer carrier on three
occasions during the same incident and placed it on the dashboard of the patrol car (General
Police Order 4.06.04) – Failed to safeguard City of Cleveland Equipment (City-issued cell

Disposition: Found “Guilty” of three Group I violations. Considering the member’s active

discharge) outweigh mitigating factors (no active discipline). The member received a Written
Reprimand for failing to safeguard City of Cleveland Equipment. This corrective action is within
the first Group I violation range of the discipline matrix when mitigating factors (no active
discipline, accepted responsibility) outweigh aggravating factors (none). The member received a
Non-disciplinary Letter of Reinstruction for removing his Wearable Camera System (WCS).

Rationale: The member accepted partial responsibility. The member did have the firearm
pointed in a safe direction; however, he placed a finger inside the trigger guard, ultimately

Hearing Held By: "Assistant Director of Public Safety, Administrative Operations"
Charge & Discipline Decision:
Specifications: Complicit in attempting to evade or defeat tax imposed by Title 26 U.S. Code and failing to pay taxes under Ohio Revised Code Section 5747 by making cash payouts totaling $7,175 to officers knowing that they were not going to receive 1099's. This conduct would constitute a crime under the laws of the United States and the State of Ohio (Manual of Rules 2.01 and 5.01) - Failed to give full disclosure in writing of business and financial relationships with security companies. The officer's role as the Special Events Coordinator did create the outside appearance of impropriety and/or a conflict of interest (General Police Order 1.3.33) – Willfully attempted to evade or defeat tax imposed by Title 26 U.S. Code and failed to pay taxes under the Ohio Revised Code Section 5747. The officer failed to claim at least $7,500 in earnings on 2017 tax statements (Manual of Rules 2.01 and 5.01) - Willfully attempted to evade or defeat tax imposed by Title 26 U.S. Code and failed to pay taxes under the Ohio Revised Code Section 5747 by claiming $15,431 in income on their 2016 tax statements when the officer received $23,289.05 in compensation after taxes. This conduct would constitute a crime under the laws of the United States and the State of Ohio (Manual of Rules 2.01 and 5.01) - Engaged in dishonest, untruthful and fraudulent behavior by failing to disclose full earnings from business entities to the federal government (General Police Order 1.07.06) - Engaged in conduct that would tend to diminish the esteem of the Division of Police or its personnel in the eyes of the public based on the investigations from outside entities (Manual of Rules 5.01). 
Disposition: Found "Not Guilty" of willfully attempting to evade or defeat tax imposed by Title 26 U.S. Code on their 2016 and 2017 taxes and being dishonest and untruthful in failing to disclose full earnings. Found "Guilty" of one Group II violation and two Group III violations. Considering the officer's active discipline history (none), the officer's appointment to Commander was revoked and the officer was demoted from the civil service rank of Captain to Lieutenant. This corrective action is within the First Group III range of the discipline matrix where aggravating factors (supervisory or command rank of the officer, multiple violations, failed to provide full and complete disclosure of financial authority) outweigh mitigating factors (no active discipline, verbally informed immediate supervisor). 
Rationale: The officer was complicit in assisting another party in attempting to evade or defeat tax imposed by Title 26 U.S. Code and failed to pay taxes under Ohio Revised Code Section 5747, by cashing a business check into his personal checking account and returning cash to another party. This conduct would constitute a crime under the laws of the United States and the State of Ohio. The officer deposited money into his personal account and immediately withdrew $7,175 dollars. This money was either income made by the officer with no tax paid or the officer assisted in paying employees cash, in which no payroll tax was paid. The officer accepted the position of Special Events Coordinator and failed to give full disclosure in writing of business and financial relationships, and his role as the Special Events Coordinator did create the outside appearance of impropriety and/or a conflict of interest. It cannot be proven that income for 2016 and 2017 was for the officer and that the officer attempted to evade or defeat taxes imposed by Title 26 U.S. Code and failed to pay taxes under the Ohio Revised Code Section 5747 since a business claimed the money as income. There are conflicting statements as to whether the officer was untruthful or grossly negligent (the latter not being the basis for criminal culpability) in failing to disclose his full earnings. The officer admitted to not providing written disclosure and full disclosure of relationships with multiple security companies as required by policy. The officer failed to take appropriate actions to eliminate the appearance of impropriety and any conflicts of interest while in his role as Special Events Coordinator. As reported by multiple event coordinators from the City of Cleveland, the officer's actions did diminish the esteem of the Division of Police and its personnel in the eyes of the public.
Charges: Arrest / Criminal Charge,Untruthfulness,Appearance of Impropriety,Ethics Violation,Conduct Unbecoming / Diminished Esteem

Rationale: The officer was derelict in his duty when he failed to intervene or report that a Grand

Ceased in assisting the complainant in obtaining legal property, ordered the
complainant’s vehicle to be towed upon learning that the complainant’s driver’s license was
suspended, refused the complainant’s request that a licensed driver be allowed to take possession
of the vehicle, and failed to provide minimal protection for the complainant’s safety when the
officer ordered the complainant off the scene on foot, leaving her vulnerable to the aggressions
of her nearby ex-boyfriend (General Police Orders 3.4.14 and 8.2.01). The officer ceased in
assisting the complainant to obtain legal property and should have allowed the vehicle to be
towed by a licensed driver. Found “Guilty” of one Group I violation. Considering the officer’s
active discipline history (none), the officer received a Written Reprimand. This corrective action
is within the First Group I violation range of the discipline matrix where mitigating factors (no
active discipline) outweigh aggravating factors (none).
Charges: Improper Tow,Neglect of Duty,Lack of Service

Hearing Held By: "Assistant Director of Public Safety, Administrative Operations"
Charge & Discipline Decision:
Failed to consider the safety of the complainant during the time on scene, to wit:
allowed the complainant’s agitated ex-boyfriend to approach and yell at the complainant as she
was seated in her vehicle a few houses down from the ex-boyfriend’s residence and again when
the officer ordered the complainant away from the scene after calling for the tow of her personal
vehicle and instructing her to walk to the gas station at the end of the street alone (Manual of
Rules 5.01. 5.08, and 5.09). As members of the Department of Public Safety, we are service
providers to the citizens of the City of Cleveland. The complainant called us for help and in turn,
she was inconvenienced and made fun of when the officer stated: “You’re sitting here talking
about lung surgery and you’re smoking how many cigarettes? You can walk up the street - it’s
healthier for you.” The officer also made unprofessional comments when speaking to the tow
truck driver on scene stating that the officer would, “solve the situation one way or another” as
the officer laughed and referenced the complainant’s citation and tow. Found “Guilty” of one
Group I violation. Considering the officer’s active discipline history (none), the officer received
a Written Reprimand. This corrective action is within the First Group I violation range of the
discipline matrix where mitigating factors (no active discipline) outweigh aggravating factors
(none).
Charges: Lack of Service,Failed to De-escalate,Unprofessional Conduct

Specification: Drove a complainant’s disabled vehicle in lieu of calling for an emergency tow
(Manual of Rules 4.01 and 5.01) – Acted defensively toward a complainant’s boyfriend and
threatened to tow the complainant’s vehicle after the boyfriend expressed his disapproval with
the manner in which the officer handled the call for service (Manual of Rules 5.09) – Failed to
activate his Wearable Camera System (WCS) until seventeen minutes into the call for service
(General Police Order 4.06.04).

Disposition: The charge of driving a complainant’s disabled vehicle in lieu of calling for an
emergency tow was dismissed. Found “Guilty” of two Group I violations. Considering the
officer’s active discipline history (3/3/2023: 2-day suspension), the officer received a Written
Reprimand. This corrective action is within the First Group I violation range of the discipline
matrix when mitigating factors (violation occurred prior to the previous discipline, accepted
responsibility for the WCS violation, circumstances where the rule was violated: aggressive
manner of the complainant) outweigh aggravating factors (active discipline).

Rationale: The officer was ordered to “Get that car out of here,” and it was imperative to get the
vehicle to a safer location and a tow had already been ordered and he mostly used the downward
slope of the bridge to coast the car into a safe and legal location. After the officer moved the
complainant’s car to a safe and legal location he engaged the complainant’s boyfriend in an
argumentative fashion, and then threatened to impound the vehicle. Further, the officer did not
have his Wearable Camera System activated for the entire incident.

Specification: Failed to investigate an assault allegation (Manual of Rules 4.01) – Failed to
request medical attention for an assault victim who was detained and requested medical attention
(Manual of Rules 4.14).

Disposition: Found “Guilty” of one Group I violation. Considering the officer’s active discipline
history (none), the officer received a written reprimand. This corrective action is within the First
Group I violation range of the discipline matrix when mitigating factors (no active discipline)
outweigh aggravating factors (none).

Rationale: The incident involved multiple locations and the officer was not in the vicinity of the
victim or the witness when they made multiple requests to provide the information; as such, the
charge of failing to investigate was dismissed. The officer did not request medical attention for
an assault victim who was detained and requested medical attention. However, because the
complainant did not suffer any visible or incapacitating injuries; the failing to request medical
attention charge more closely comports with a Group I violation (conduct that has a negative
impact on the operations or professional image of the Division or negatively impacts
relationships with other officers, agencies or the public) and was amended from a Group II
violation to a Group I violation.
Charges: Lack of Service,Failed to Request Medical Attention

Charge & Discipline Decision:

officer (General Police Order 1.1.08 and Manual of Rules 2.02, 2.04 and 2.13).

Management Program through Ease at Work. This corrective action is within the First Group I
violation range of the discipline matrix when mitigating factors (no active discipline, accepted
responsibility) outweigh aggravating factors (none).
Rationale: The officer pled “No Contest” and accepted responsibility. The officer did engage in
a physical altercation with another officer, which was limited to the officers shoving each other
with no injuries.

Specifications: Failed to arrest the suspect of a Domestic Violence and Rape despite having
probable cause (General Police Order 5.05.01 and Manual of Rules 4.01) – Failed to assume
responsibility of a scene involving a Rape and Domestic Violence suspect by deferring all
judgment and responsibility to a less senior patrol officer (Manual of Rules 4.19).
Disposition: The charge of failing to arrest the suspect of a Domestic Violence and Rape was
dismissed. The charge of failing to assume responsibility of a scene more closely comports with
a Group I Violation (conduct that has a negative impact on the operations or professional image
of the Division or negatively impacts relationships with other officers, agencies or the public) –
the charge was amended from a Group II violation to a Group I violation. Found “Guilty” of one 
Group I violation. Considering the officer’s active discipline history (none), the officer received
a Written Reprimand. This corrective action is within the First Group I violation range of the
discipline matrix when mitigating factors (no active discipline) outweigh aggravating factors
(none).
Rationale: While the officer did fail to arrest the suspect while having probable cause to do so,
he did contact a supervisor for guidance and the supervisor ultimately steered the officer away
from the decision to arrest. This was the officer’s assignment and he did defer all judgment and
responsibility to a less senior patrol officer.

Hearing Held By: Deputy Chief of Homeland Special Operations
Charge & Discipline Decision:
Specification: Used excessive vulgar language numerous times (Manual of Rules 5.09).

Disposition: Found “Guilty” of one Group I violation. Considering the member’s active
discipline history (none) the member received a Written Reprimand. This corrective action is
within the first Group I violation range of the discipline matrix when mitigating factors (no
active discipline, intense situation with an irrational male wielding what appeared to be an
edged weapon) outweigh aggravating factors (none).

Rationale: The member pled “No Contest” and accepted responsibility. The member’s language,
especially after the suspect tossed the weapon away and was complying, “Get the fuck down; get
the fuck down, motherfucker,” which was made in the presence of a crowd, is outside of
Division expectations.

Specification: Operated a Cleveland Police vehicle that was involved in a motor vehicle crash
that was reviewed by the Traffic Safety Analysis Panel (TSAP) and determined to be preventable
(Manual of Rules 4.03, 6.03 and 6.05).

Disposition: Found “Guilty” of one Group I violation. Considering the member’s active
discipline history (none), the member received a Written Reprimand. This corrective action is
within the first Group I violation range of the discipline matrix when mitigating factors (accepted
responsibility, no active discipline) outweigh aggravating factors (none).

Rationale: The member pled “No Contest” and accepted responsibility. This is the member’s
second preventable motor vehicle accident within two (2) years.

Specification: Did not enter the store and confer with the employee who was concerned about
multiple individuals standing outside of the store with a firearm (Manual of Rules 4.18).

Disposition: Found “Guilty” of one Group I violation. Considering the member’s active
discipline history (none), the member received a Written Reprimand. This corrective action is
within the first Group I violation range of the discipline matrix when mitigating factors (no
active discipline; indicated that since a debriefing he is aware of the proper way to handle such
assignments) outweigh aggravating factors (none).

Rationale: The member responded to a radio assignment prompted by the owner of a store
regarding an employee’s concerns of multiple individuals standing outside of the store with a
firearm. Instead of conferring with the employee, the member only spoke with those outside of
the store before leaving.

Specification: Did not enter the store and confer with the employee who was concerned about
multiple individuals standing outside of the store with a firearm (Manual of Rules 4.18).
Disposition: Found “Guilty” of one Group I violation. Considering the member’s active
discipline history (none), the member received a Written Reprimand. This corrective action is
within the first Group I violation range of the discipline matrix when mitigating factors (no
active discipline; indicated that since a debriefing he is aware of the proper way to handle such
assignments) outweigh aggravating factors (none).
Rationale: The member responded to a radio assignment prompted by the owner of a store
regarding an employee’s concerns of multiple individuals standing outside of the store with a
firearm. Instead of conferring with the employee, the member only spoke with those outside of
the store before leaving.

Specifications: Failed to report a Level 2 use of force (General Police Order 2.01.05) –
Discourteous in speech (Manual of Rules 5.09).
Disposition: Found “Guilty” of two Group I violations. Considering the member’s active
discipline history (none) the member received a Written Reprimand. This corrective action is
within the first Group I violation range of the discipline matrix when mitigating factors (no
active discipline, use of force was within policy, accepted partial responsibility) outweigh
aggravating factors (11/18/2024: Verbal Counseling – Discourteous).
Rationale: The member accepted responsibility for his delay in reporting the use of force when
he was unaware that he had used body weight on the suspect. Upon learning that he had used
force, he completed the required BlueTeam entry. The use of force was within policy and after
the suspect was hand-cuffed, the member stated the following: “You’re going to jail playboy,”
“Get your little ass up,” “Shut the fuck up. Shut the fuck up and get in the car.” This language is
outside of Division expectations.

Specification: Wearable Camera System (WCS) violation (failed to reactivate) (General Police
Order 4.06.04).
Disposition: Found “Guilty” of one Group I violation. Considering the member’s active
discipline history (none) the member received a Written Reprimand. This corrective action is
within the first Group I violation range of the discipline matrix when mitigating factors (no
active discipline) outweigh aggravating factors (2/5/2024: Verbal Counseling – WCS violation).
Rationale: The member did have his Wearable Camera System activated, but returned his
camera to “Buffering Mode” and then failed to reactivate his Wearable Camera System while
assisting with crowd control and witnessing a Level 2 use of force.

Specification: Delayed reporting a level 2 use of force (General Police Order 2.01.05).
Disposition: Found “Guilty” of one Group I violation. Considering the member’s active
discipline history (none) the member received a Written Reprimand. This corrective action is
within the First Group I violation range of the discipline matrix when mitigating factors (no
active discipline, use of force was within policy, he did convey the subject to the hospital for
evaluation) outweigh aggravating factors (delays in use of force reporting may cause a loss of
evidence and create an appearance of impropriety).
Rationale: The subject complained that she was pregnant, couldn’t breathe and was suffering
chest pain. These medical conditions are incongruent with the force used and were not caused by
the use of force. However, because force was used, and these statements were made, the member
should have had a supervisor respond to the scene or the hospital.

Specification: Failed to complete and forward an injury investigation in a timely manner
(General Police Order 1.1.13).
Disposition: Found “Guilty” of one Group I violation. Considering the member’s active
discipline history (none) the member received a Written Reprimand. This corrective action is
within the First Group I violation range of the discipline matrix when mitigating factors
(accepted responsibility, no active discipline) outweigh aggravating factors (supervisory rank,
Verbal Counseling issued on 2/7/2024 for late BlueTeam investigations).
Rationale: While there was an issue regarding the OH-1 Crash report, it did not justify the
months-long delay in forwarding the injury packet. It is essential that injury packets are
forwarded in a timely manner, as delays in the investigation may also delay Bureau of Workers'
Compensation approval of medical treatment.

Specification: Abusive and demeaning language (Manual of Rules 5.09).

Disposition: The charge more closely comports with a Group I Violation (conduct that has a
negative impact on the operations or professional image of the Division or that negatively
impacts relationships with other officers, agencies or the public); the charge was amended from a
Group II violation to a Group I violation. Found “Guilty” of one Group I violation. Considering
the member’s active discipline history (none) the member received a Written Reprimand. This
corrective action is within the First Group I violation range of the discipline matrix when
mitigating factors (no active discipline, use of force was within policy) outweigh aggravating
factors (none).

Rationale: During a foot pursuit that concluded with a use of force, the member directed an
expletive filled remark at the fleeing suspect. The language the member used was outside of
Division expectations and in conflict with Manual of Rules 5.09.

Specification: Diminished the esteem (contacted the Communication Control Section (CCS)
while intoxicated) (Manual of Rules 5.01).

Disposition: The charge more closely comports with a Group I Violation (conduct that has a
negative impact on the operations or professional image of the Division or that negatively
impacts relationships with other officers, agencies or the public); the charge was amended from a
Group II violation to a Group I violation. Found “Guilty” of one Group I violation. Considering
the member’s active discipline history (none) the member received a Written Reprimand. This
corrective action is within the First Group I Violation range of the discipline matrix when
mitigating factors (no active discipline at the time of the hearing) outweigh aggravating factors
(none).

Rationale: The member did contact the Communication Control Section (CCS) while apparently
intoxicated and requesting assistance from the Cleveland Division of Police; she did provide the
name “Jennifer” and stated, “See you guys in four hours.” Her call had the potential to cause
unwarranted concern on the part of responding officers, as they would be looking for a victim
named “Jennifer.”

a supervisor (General Police Order 8.1.01).
Disposition: Found “Guilty” of two Group I violations. Considering the member’s active

Hearing Held By: Deputy Chief of Homeland Special Operations,Deputy Chief of Administrative Operations
Charge & Discipline Decision:
Specifications: Lack of service (Manual of Rules 5.01, 5.02, 5.08 and 5.09) – Unprofessional
conduct/behavior (Manual of Rules 5.01, 5.02, 5.08 and 5.09).
Disposition: The unprofessional conduct/behavior charge was merged with the lack of service
charge. Found “Guilty” of one Group I violation. Considering the member’s active discipline
history (none), the member received a Written Reprimand. This corrective action is within the First Group I violation range of the discipline matrix when mitigating factors (no active
discipline) outweigh aggravating factors (none).
Rationale: The two specifications are from the same incident and are similar. In an Amended
Findings Letter (dated: 12/22/2025) from the Office of Professional Standards and the Civilian
Police Review Board, “...the Board modified its finding for Specification #2 and merged it with
Specification #1 but maintaining the recommendation for disciplinary action consistent with a
Group level #1 Violation.” The member was not the primary officer and was assisting on this
assignment (helping to control a chaotic scene and guarding the suspect who had been placed
into the rear of a patrol car). When confronted by the victim, who was approaching the member
and the in-custody suspect, the member had the victim return across the street for eventual
interview by the primary officer, including the statement that he would be handcuffed if he did
not comply.

Specification: Wearable Camera System (WCS) violation (General Police Order 4.06.04).

Disposition: Found “Guilty” of one Group I violation. Considering the member’s active
discipline history (none) the member received a Written Reprimand. This corrective action is
within the Group I violation range of the discipline matrix when mitigating factors (self-reported
violation, responding to assist an officer, assisting with staffing in a different district) outweigh
aggravating factors (WCS not on person, active discipline).

Rationale: The member failed to activate his Wearable Camera System (WCS) prior to
